topics.title: Existence, Uniqueness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE -1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Existence, Uniqueness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for GDMAT101, the **Existence and Uniqueness Theorem** (also widely known as **Picard's Theorem** or the **Picard–Lindelöf Theorem**) is a foundational result in the theory of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) of the form:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y), \quad y(x_0) = y_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement (Picard's Existence and Uniqueness Theorem):**
> Let $R = \{(x, y) : \vert x - x_0 \vert \le a,\ \vert y - y_0 \vert \le b\}$ be a closed rectangular region in the $xy$-plane containing the point $(x_0, y_0)$. If:
> 1. **Continuity Condition:** $f(x, y)$ is continuous on $R$.
> 2. **Lipschitz Condition:** $f(x, y)$ satisfies a Lipschitz condition with respect to $y$ on $R$; that is, there exists a constant $L > 0$ such that
> $$\vert f(x, y_1) - f(x, y_2) \vert \le L \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$$
> for all $(x, y_1), (x, y_2) \in R$.
>
> Then, the initial value problem (IVP) has **at least one** solution on some interval $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$, and this solution is **unique**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tracking the **growth of a bacteria colony** in a petri dish. You measure the initial population $y_0$ at time $x_0 = 0$. The rate of change $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ describes *how fast* the population grows depending on time and current population.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Key Questions a Biologist Asks:**
> 1. **Existence:** *"Does a predictable growth curve exist at all from my starting point?"* — This is guaranteed as long as the growth rate function $f(x, y)$ behaves nicely (is **continuous**) — no sudden jumps or undefined behaviors.
> 2. **Uniqueness:** *"Is the future of my colony uniquely determined, or could it branch into many possible futures depending on tiny fluctuations?"* — This is guaranteed only if $f$ does not change too wildly with $y$ (the **Lipschitz condition**).

Think of it like a river flowing through a landscape: if the slope (derivative) is continuous and doesn't have vertical cliffs, the path of a tiny leaf dropped at any point is **both guaranteed to exist and to be unique**.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standards

- **Lipschitz constant $L$**: A strictly positive real number that bounds the rate of change of $f$ with respect to $y$.
- **Interval of existence $h$**: Given by $h = \min\left(a, \dfrac{b}{M}\right)$, where $M = \max \vert f(x, y) \vert$ on $R$.
- **Sufficient (alternative) condition**: If $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ is continuous and bounded on $R$ with $\left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \right\vert \le L$, then $f$ is Lipschitz in $y$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Theorem — Structured Logic

### Step 1: Re-write the IVP in Integral Form
The differential equation $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$ can be equivalently expressed as an **integral equation**:

$$y(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y(t))\, dt$$

This transformation is the heart of **Picard's iterative method**.

### Step 2: Construct the Picard Iterates
Starting with $y_0(x) = y_0$, we define a sequence:

$$y_{n+1}(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y_n(t))\, dt, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

This sequence **converges uniformly** on the interval $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$ to the unique solution $y(x)$.

### Step 3: Why Both Conditions Are Needed
- **Continuity** of $f$ alone guarantees **existence** (by Peano's existence theorem), but **NOT uniqueness**.
- The **Lipschitz condition** tightens this to a **unique** solution, ruling out the possibility of multiple solution curves passing through $(x_0, y_0)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-example for why Lipschitz is needed:** The IVP $\frac{dy}{dx} = \sqrt{\vert y \vert}$, $y(0) = 0$, has $f(x, y) = \sqrt{\vert y \vert}$ which is continuous but not Lipschitz at $y = 0$. It admits **two** solutions: $y = 0$ and $y = x^2/4$. This is a KTU-favorite trick question!

### Step 4: Engineering / Life Science Utility
In life sciences, this theorem justifies:
- Why **deterministic population models** (Malthusian, Logistic) yield **single, predictable trajectories** from initial conditions.
- Why **epidemic models** (SIR models) provide reliable forecasts under smooth transmission-rate assumptions.
- Why **pharmacokinetic models** can uniquely predict drug concentration over time.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Notes / Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| General First-Order IVP | $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y),\ y(x_0) = y_0$ | Standard form for KTU problems |
| Integral (Picard) Form | $y(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y(t))\, dt$ | Equivalent to ODE + IC |
| Picard Iterates | $y_{n+1}(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y_n(t))\, dt$ | Start with $y_0(x) = y_0$ |
| Continuity Condition | $f(x, y)$ continuous on $R$ | Guarantees **existence** |
| Lipschitz Condition | $\vert f(x, y_1) - f(x, y_2) \vert \le L \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | Guarantees **uniqueness** |
| Mean Value Bound (Sufficient) | $\left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \right\vert \le L$ on $R$ | Implies Lipschitz condition |
| Max Bound on $f$ | $M = \max_{(x,y) \in R} \vert f(x, y) \vert$ | Used to find $h$ |
| Interval of Existence | $h = \min\left(a,\ \dfrac{b}{M}\right)$ | Where unique solution lives |
| Uniform Convergence Bound | $\vert y_{n+1}(x) - y_n(x) \vert \le \dfrac{M L^n \vert x - x_0 \vert^{n+1}}{(n+1)!}$ | Geometric-style convergence |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all of the above expressions, the absolute value bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid conflicts with markdown table syntax. Do **not** write them as `|y|` inside KTU cheat-sheet tables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Deriving the Interval of Existence $h$

**Goal:** Show that $h = \min\left(a, \dfrac{b}{M}\right)$ ensures the Picard iterates remain within $R$.

**Step 1:** We begin by assuming $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le a$, so the integration interval is bounded by $a$. This ensures $(t, y_n(t)) \in R$ provided $y_n(t)$ stays close to $y_0$.

**Step 2:** Define the strip $S = \{(x, y) : \vert x - x_0 \vert \le a,\ \vert y - y_0 \vert \le b\}$. We require $\vert y(x) - y_0 \vert \le b$ on this strip. Starting from the integral form:

$$\vert y(x) - y_0 \vert = \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y(t))\, dt \right\vert$$

**Step 3:** Apply the absolute value property of integrals (since $f$ is continuous, the integral is bounded by $M$ times the length of the integration interval):

$$\vert y(x) - y_0 \vert \le \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} \vert f(t, y(t)) \vert\, dt \right\vert \le M \cdot \vert x - x_0 \vert$$

**Step 4:** To guarantee $\vert y(x) - y_0 \vert \le b$, we need:

$$M \cdot \vert x - x_0 \vert \le b \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert x - x_0 \vert \le \frac{b}{M}$$

**Step 5:** Taking the intersection of both constraints $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le a$ and $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le \dfrac{b}{M}$ gives the final result:

$$h = \min\left(a, \frac{b}{M}\right)$$

This guarantees that **every** iterate $y_n(x)$ remains inside the rectangle $R$ for $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$, ensuring the iteration is well-defined.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Showing Lipschitz + Continuity Implies Convergence

**Setup:** Let $y_n(x)$ and $y_{n+1}(x)$ be two successive Picard iterates on $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$.

**Step 1:** Subtract successive iterates:

$$y_{n+1}(x) - y_n(x) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \left[ f(t, y_n(t)) - f(t, y_{n-1}(t)) \right] dt$$

**Step 2:** Take absolute values:

$$\vert y_{n+1}(x) - y_n(x) \vert \le \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} \vert f(t, y_n(t)) - f(t, y_{n-1}(t)) \vert\, dt \right\vert$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Lipschitz condition** with constant $L$:

$$\vert f(t, y_n(t)) - f(t, y_{n-1}(t)) \vert \le L \vert y_n(t) - y_{n-1}(t) \vert$$

So we get:

$$\vert y_{n+1}(x) - y_n(x) \vert \le L \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} \vert y_n(t) - y_{n-1}(t) \vert\, dt \right\vert$$

**Step 4:** Use mathematical induction. The base case $n = 0$:

$$\vert y_1(x) - y_0(x) \vert = \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y_0)\, dt \right\vert \le M \vert x - x_0 \vert \le M h$$

**Step 5:** Inductive hypothesis: Assume for some $n \ge 1$:

$$\vert y_n(x) - y_{n-1}(x) \vert \le \frac{M L^{n-1} \vert x - x_0 \vert^{n}}{n!}$$

**Step 6:** Inductive step — using the integral bound:

$$\vert y_{n+1}(x) - y_n(x) \vert \le L \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{M L^{n-1} \vert t - x_0 \vert^{n}}{n!}\, dt = \frac{M L^n \vert x - x_0 \vert^{n+1}}{(n+1)!}$$

This confirms the bound by induction.

**Step 7:** Geometric series comparison. Since $L h \le M$ (under our choice of $h$), we have $\dfrac{L^n h^n}{n!} \le \dfrac{(M h)^n}{n!}$, and:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(M h)^n}{n!} = e^{M h}$$

This sum converges, so by the **Weierstrass M-test**, $\{y_n(x)\}$ converges **uniformly** to a continuous limit function $y(x)$ on $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$.

**Step 8:** Uniqueness. Suppose $y(x)$ and $z(x)$ are two solutions with $y(x_0) = z(x_0) = y_0$. Then:

$$\vert y(x) - z(x) \vert = \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} [f(t, y(t)) - f(t, z(t))]\, dt \right\vert \le L \left\vert \int_{x_0}^{x} \vert y(t) - z(t) \vert\, dt \right\vert$$

By **Gronwall's inequality** (or elementary bound), since $y(x_0) = z(x_0)$, we conclude $y(x) = z(x)$ on the interval. **Uniqueness is proven.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (Existence Interval Computation)

**Problem:** Given the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y^2$ with $y(0) = 0$, find the lower bound $h$ for the existence interval on the rectangle $R: \vert x \vert \le 1,\ \vert y \vert \le 1$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$, $a = 1$, $b = 1$.

**Step 2:** Find the maximum $M$ of $\vert f(x, y) \vert$ on $R$:

$$M = \max_{\vert x \vert \le 1,\, \vert y \vert \le 1} (x^2 + y^2) = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$$

**Step 3:** Compute $h$:

$$h = \min\left(a,\ \frac{b}{M}\right) = \min\left(1,\ \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4:** So a unique solution exists for $\vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{2}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Existence & Uniqueness Theorem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical dependencies between the hypotheses and conclusions of Picard's theorem, which is a high-yield visual for KTU Module 1 conceptual questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initial Value Problem: dy/dx = f(x,y), y(x0) = y0"] --> B["Define Rectangular Region R"]
    B --> C{"Is f(x,y) continuous on R?"}
    C -- "YES" --> D["Continuity Condition SATISFIED"]
    C -- "NO" --> E["Theorem NOT applicable"]
    D --> F{"Is f Lipschitz in y? OR is partial f / partial y bounded?"}
    F -- "YES" --> G["Lipschitz Condition SATISFIED"]
    F -- "NO" --> H["Only EXISTENCE guaranteed (Peano)"]
    G --> I["Compute M = max absolute value of f on R"]
    I --> J["Compute h = min of a and b divided by M"]
    J --> K["UNIQUE solution y(x) exists on absolute value of x - x0 less than or equal to h"]
    H --> L["Multiple solutions may exist - uniqueness FAILS"]
    K --> M["Apply Picard Iterates to approximate y(x)"]
    L --> N["Counter-example: dy/dx = sqrt absolute value of y, y(0) = 0"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Picard Iteration Engine

This block diagram shows the iterative machinery used to numerically construct the unique solution in real-world ODE solvers used in computational biology.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        I1["Initial Guess: y0 of x = y0 constant"]
        I2["Function f of x, y"]
        I3["Initial Condition x0, y0"]
    end

    subgraph ITER["PICARD ITERATION CORE"]
        P1["y1 of x = y0 + integral from x0 to x of f of t, y0 of t dt"]
        P2["y2 of x = y0 + integral from x0 to x of f of t, y1 of t dt"]
        P3["y3 of x = y0 + integral from x0 to x of f of t, y2 of t dt"]
        P4["yn plus 1 of x = y0 + integral from x0 to x of f of t, yn of t dt"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        O1["Uniform Convergence Check via Weierstrass M-Test"]
        O2["Unique Solution y of x on interval h"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Verifying Both Conditions for a Given IVP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Given dy/dx = f(x,y), y(x0) = y0"] --> T1["Step 1: Identify f, x0, y0, rectangle R"]
    T1 --> T2["Step 2: Check if f is continuous on R using partial derivatives and elementary functions"]
    T2 -- "Continuous" --> T3["Step 3: Compute partial f / partial y on R"]
    T2 -- "Not Continuous" --> Z["STOP: Theorem fails"]
    T3 --> T4{"Is partial f / partial y bounded on R?"}
    T4 -- "YES" --> T5["L = max of absolute value of partial f / partial y"]
    T4 -- "NO" --> T6["Try direct Lipschitz verification from definition"]
    T6 --> T7{"Does there exist L such that absolute value of f of x, y1 minus f of x, y2 is at most L times absolute value of y1 minus y2?"}
    T7 -- "YES" --> T5
    T7 -- "NO" --> Y["Only existence, NOT uniqueness"]
    T5 --> T8["Compute M = max of absolute value of f on R"]
    T8 --> T9["Compute h = min of a and b divided by M"]
    T9 --> W["CONCLUSION: Unique solution exists on absolute value of x - x0 less than or equal to h"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**State the Picard–Lindelöf existence and uniqueness theorem for the initial value problem $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y),\ y(x_0) = y_0$. What role does the Lipschitz condition play in the theorem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Statement: If $f(x, y)$ is **continuous** in a closed rectangular region $R = \{\vert x - x_0 \vert \le a,\ \vert y - y_0 \vert \le b\}$ containing $(x_0, y_0)$, AND satisfies a **Lipschitz condition** in $y$ on $R$ (i.e., $\vert f(x, y_1) - f(x, y_2) \vert \le L \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ for some $L > 0$), then the IVP has a **unique solution** on $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$ where $h = \min(a, b/M)$.
- **(1 mark)** Role of continuity: ensures **existence** of at least one solution (via Peano's theorem).
- **(1 mark)** Role of Lipschitz condition: ensures **uniqueness** by preventing multiple solution curves from passing through the same initial point.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Give a counter-example to show that continuity of $f(x, y)$ alone is not sufficient for uniqueness of solutions of $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y),\ y(x_0) = y_0$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Consider the IVP: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \sqrt{\vert y \vert},\ y(0) = 0$.
- **(1 mark)** Here, $f(x, y) = \sqrt{\vert y \vert}$ is continuous on $R$ containing the origin (continuity ✓), but $\partial f / \partial y = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{\vert y \vert}}$ becomes **unbounded** as $y \to 0$, so the Lipschitz condition fails.
- **(1 mark)** The IVP has **two** distinct solutions: $y_1(x) = 0$ and $y_2(x) = x^2 / 4$, proving that continuity alone is not sufficient for uniqueness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** For the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y^2,\ y(0) = 0$, verify the existence and uniqueness theorem on the rectangle $R: \vert x \vert \le 1,\ \vert y \vert \le 1$. Find the lower bound $h$ for the interval of existence.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using Picard's iteration method, compute the first two successive approximations $y_1(x)$ and $y_2(x)$ for the IVP in part (a).

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify $f(x, y) = x + y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$, $a = 1$, $b = 1$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Continuity check:** $f(x, y) = x + y^2$ is a polynomial in $x$ and $y$, hence continuous everywhere, in particular on $R = \{\vert x \vert \le 1,\ \vert y \vert \le 1\}$. ✓

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Lipschitz (via $\partial f / \partial y$) check:** $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2y$. On $R$, $\vert 2y \vert \le 2 \cdot 1 = 2$. So $L = 2$. Since $\partial f / \partial y$ is continuous and bounded on $R$, $f$ is **Lipschitz in $y$** on $R$. ✓

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $M$:
$$M = \max_{\vert x \vert \le 1,\, \vert y \vert \le 1} \vert x + y^2 \vert = \vert 1 + 1 \vert = 2$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $h$:
$$h = \min\left(1,\ \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** A **unique solution** exists for $\vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{2}$.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Initial approximation $y_0(x) = y_0 = 0$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** First Picard iterate $y_1(x)$:
$$y_1(x) = y_0 + \int_{0}^{x} f(t, y_0(t))\, dt = 0 + \int_{0}^{x} (t + 0^2)\, dt = \int_{0}^{x} t\, dt = \frac{x^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Second Picard iterate $y_2(x)$:
$$y_2(x) = y_0 + \int_{0}^{x} f(t, y_1(t))\, dt = \int_{0}^{x} \left(t + \left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right)^2\right) dt = \int_{0}^{x} \left(t + \frac{t^4}{4}\right) dt$$

**Step 4 (final computation):**
$$y_2(x) = \frac{t^2}{2}\bigg|_0^x + \frac{t^5}{20}\bigg|_0^x = \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^5}{20}$$

**Final Answer:** $y_1(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{2}$, $\quad y_2(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{2} + \dfrac{x^5}{20}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** State and explain the existence and uniqueness theorem (Picard's theorem) for the initial value problem. Show that $f(x, y) = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ satisfies the hypothesis on $R = \{\vert x \vert \le 1,\ \vert y \vert \le 1\}$ with $y(0) = 0$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Find the Lipschitz constant $L$ and the lower bound $h$ for the interval of existence of the unique solution of the IVP in part (a).

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** **Statement:** If $f(x, y)$ is continuous on a closed rectangle $R$ containing $(x_0, y_0)$ and satisfies a Lipschitz condition $\vert f(x, y_1) - f(x, y_2) \vert \le L \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ in $y$ on $R$, then a unique solution $y(x)$ to the IVP exists on $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$ where $h = \min(a, b/M)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Continuity of $f$:** $f(x, y) = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ is a composition of continuous functions (polynomial inside, square root outside, both continuous for $x^2 + y^2 \ge 0$). Hence $f$ is continuous on $R$. ✓

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Lipschitz (via bounded partial):**
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}}$$

On $R$ (excluding the origin, but bounded everywhere by $\le 1$ in absolute value), we have $\left\vert \dfrac{y}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}} \right\vert \le 1$. So $\partial f / \partial y$ is continuous and bounded, hence $f$ is Lipschitz in $y$. ✓

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** **Lipschitz constant:**
$$L = \max_{(x,y) \in R} \left\vert \frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}} \right\vert = 1$$

(achieved at points like $(0, \pm 1)$ on the boundary of $R$).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Compute $M$:**
$$M = \max_{\vert x \vert \le 1,\, \vert y \vert \le 1} \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Compute $h$:** with $a = 1$, $b = 1$:
$$h = \min\left(1,\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** A unique solution exists for $\vert x \vert \le \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary of $R$:** Many students compute $M$ using interior values only. Always evaluate $M$ on the **closed** rectangle, including the boundary (corners often give the maximum).
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $M$:** $L$ bounds $\partial f / \partial y$, while $M$ bounds $f$ itself. They are entirely different quantities. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the counter-example question:** Q2 in Part A is a KTU favorite. Memorize the **$\sqrt{\vert y \vert}$ example** as a standard counter-example.
> 4. **Writing absolute values as `|x|` in tables:** In KTU answer scripts, this is fine, but in this cheat-sheet style markdown, use `\vert x \vert` to avoid formatting errors.
> 5. **Assuming $\partial f / \partial y$ continuous = Lipschitz:** This is a **sufficient** condition, not the definition. Always mention that boundedness + continuity of $\partial f / \partial y$ **implies** Lipschitz — do not state it as an "if and only if."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Picard's Theorem** is the bedrock result for the IVP $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y),\ y(x_0) = y_0$.
- ✅ **Two conditions**: continuity (for existence) + Lipschitz in $y$ (for uniqueness).
- ✅ **Sufficient for Lipschitz**: $\partial f / \partial y$ continuous and bounded on the rectangle $R$.
- ✅ **Counter-example**: $\frac{dy}{dx} = \sqrt{\vert y \vert},\ y(0) = 0$ — continuous but not Lipschitz at origin → non-unique solutions $y = 0$ and $y = x^2/4$.
- ✅ **Interval of existence**: $h = \min\left(a,\ \dfrac{b}{M}\right)$, where $M = \max \vert f \vert$ on $R$.
- ✅ **Lipschitz constant $L$**: typically $L = \max \vert \partial f / \partial y \vert$ on $R$.
- ✅ **Picard iterates**: $y_{n+1}(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f(t, y_n(t))\, dt$, starting from $y_0(x) = y_0$.
- ✅ **Convergence** of iterates is **uniform** on $\vert x - x_0 \vert \le h$ by the Weierstrass M-test.
- ✅ **Uniqueness proof** uses the Gronwall-style argument: $\vert y - z \vert \le L \int \vert y - z \vert\, dt \Rightarrow y = z$.
- ✅ **Life science application**: validates deterministic models (Malthus, Logistic, SIR) in biology, ecology, and epidemiology.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for KTU exams**: **"CC + LC = EU"** → **C**ontinuity + **L**ipschitz **C**ondition = **E**xistence + **U**niqueness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 1 — FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM FOR LINEAR SYSTEMS & THE MATRIX EIGENVALUE PROBLEM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Fundamental Theorem for Linear Systems (Existence & Uniqueness)

A **system of first-order linear differential equations** in matrix form is written as:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{X}(t)$ is an $n \times 1$ state vector (the unknown functions)
- $A(t)$ is an $n \times n$ coefficient matrix
- $\mathbf{F}(t)$ is an $n \times 1$ forcing (input) vector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM):**
> Let $\mathbf{X}(t_0) = \mathbf{X}_0$ be an initial condition. If every entry of $A(t)$ and $\mathbf{F}(t)$ is **continuous on an open interval $I$ containing $t_0$**, then the initial value problem (IVP)
> $$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t), \quad \mathbf{X}(t_0)=\mathbf{X}_0$$
> possesses **exactly one solution**, and that solution is defined on the **entire interval $I$**.

### 1.2 Homogeneous vs. Non-Homogeneous Linear Systems

| System Type | Mathematical Form | Forcing Term | Biological Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous** | $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X}$ | $\mathbf{F}(t) = \mathbf{0}$ | Closed ecosystem with no external species input — population evolves purely from internal dynamics |
| **Non-Homogeneous** | $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t)$ | $\mathbf{F}(t) \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Ecosystem receiving external immigration of species — natural growth PLUS an outside source |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Population Pond" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a pond with two fish species. The first equation says *"the rate at which species X grows depends on its own population and on species Y's population through matrix $A$."* The forcing vector $\mathbf{F}(t)$ is like **rain** — sometimes it falls (non-homogeneous), sometimes it doesn't (homogeneous). The Fundamental Theorem guarantees that, as long as the "weather" (the entries of $A$ and $\mathbf{F}$) is well-behaved (continuous), there is **one and only one** future for the pond given its starting state. No two different futures are possible from the same initial snapshot.

### 1.4 The Matrix Eigenvalue Problem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (EIGENVALUE PROBLEM):**
> Let $A$ be a square $n \times n$ matrix. A scalar $\lambda$ is called an **eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a **non-zero column vector** $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (or $\mathbb{C}^n$) such that
> $$A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$
> The non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ is called an **eigenvector** of $A$ corresponding to $\lambda$.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretching Rubber Sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> Picture a square rubber sheet on which you draw a circle. Now stretch it in some specific direction by matrix $A$. Most vectors (arrows drawn on the sheet) get **rotated AND scaled**. Eigenvectors are the *very special* arrows that, after the stretching, point in **exactly the same direction** as before — they were only **multiplied in length** by the scalar $\lambda$. The eigenvalue $\lambda$ is simply that **scaling factor** (e.g., $\lambda = 3$ means "stretched to 3 times its original length"; $\lambda = -2$ means "flipped and stretched to 2 times").

### 1.6 Physical & Biological Constants Referenced in This Module

- **Population doubling rate** is conceptually analogous to $\ln(\lambda)$ for $\lambda > 1$
- **Decay constant** of a radioactive isotope is $-\ln(\lambda)$ when $0 < \lambda < 1$
- The trace of $A$ equals the sum of eigenvalues: $\operatorname{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i$
- The determinant equals the product of eigenvalues: $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i$

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Fundamental Theorem

### Pillar 1 — Existence
There is **at least one** solution curve $\mathbf{X}(t)$ on interval $I$ that starts at $\mathbf{X}_0$ at time $t_0$.

### Pillar 2 — Uniqueness
There is **at most one** such curve — no two distinct solution trajectories can pass through the same initial point.

### Pillar 3 — Domain of Validity
The interval $I$ is determined by where $A(t)$ and $\mathbf{F}(t)$ remain continuous; the theorem does **not** guarantee the solution extends beyond a discontinuity (a "blow-up" point).

## 2.2 Structural Consequences for Homogeneous vs. Non-Homogeneous Systems

**For a Homogeneous System** $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X}$ with $\mathbf{X}(t_0) = \mathbf{0}$:
- The **trivial solution** $\mathbf{X}(t) \equiv \mathbf{0}$ is always a solution.
- Uniqueness guarantees $\mathbf{X}(t) \equiv \mathbf{0}$ is the **only** solution starting from $\mathbf{0}$.
- The set of **all** solutions forms an $n$-dimensional vector space called the **general solution space**.

**For a Non-Homogeneous System** $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t)$:
- The trivial solution is **not** a solution (since $\mathbf{F} \neq \mathbf{0}$).
- General solution = **complementary function** (homogeneous part) + **particular integral**:
$$\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{X}_c(t) + \mathbf{X}_p(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** For a constant-coefficient system $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\mathbf{X}$, a fundamental matrix $\Phi(t)$ is any $n \times n$ matrix whose columns are $n$ linearly independent solutions. The general solution is $\mathbf{X}(t) = \Phi(t)\,\mathbf{C}$ for constant vector $\mathbf{C}$.

## 2.3 The Characteristic Equation (Heart of the Eigenvalue Problem)

Starting from $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$, we rewrite:
$$A\mathbf{v} - \lambda \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; (A - \lambda I)\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

For a non-trivial solution $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ to exist, the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ **must be singular** (non-invertible), i.e., its determinant must vanish:
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This determinant, when expanded, yields a polynomial of degree $n$ in $\lambda$ — the **characteristic polynomial** $p(\lambda)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Eigenvalue Equation | $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Definition |
| 2 | Characteristic Equation | $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | Find eigenvalues |
| 3 | Characteristic Polynomial (degree $n$) | $p(\lambda) = (-1)^n \lambda^n + \cdots + \det(A)$ | Expand to get polynomial |
| 4 | Eigenvector Equation | $(A - \lambda I)\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ | Find eigenvectors from null space |
| 5 | Trace-Eigenvalue Identity | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \operatorname{tr}(A)$ | Quick check (sum of diagonal) |
| 6 | Determinant-Eigenvalue Identity | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$ | Quick check (product) |
| 7 | Algebraic Multiplicity | $\operatorname{am}(\lambda_i) = $ multiplicity of root in $p(\lambda)$ | Dimension check |
| 8 | Geometric Multiplicity | $\operatorname{gm}(\lambda_i) = \dim \ker(A - \lambda_i I) = n - \operatorname{rank}(A - \lambda_i I)$ | Independent eigenvectors count |
| 9 | Linear Independence Rule | $1 \le \operatorname{gm}(\lambda_i) \le \operatorname{am}(\lambda_i)$ | Determines if $A$ is diagonalizable |
| 10 | Diagonalization | $A = P D P^{-1}$ where $D = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$ and $P = [\mathbf{v}_1 \cdots \mathbf{v}_n]$ | Decouples the system |
| 11 | Solution of Constant-Coeff. System | $\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_n e^{\lambda_n t}\mathbf{v}_n$ | When eigenvalues are distinct |
| 12 | Fundamental Theorem Condition | $A(t), \mathbf{F}(t)$ continuous on $I \ni t_0$ | Guarantees existence & uniqueness |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** $\det(A - \lambda I)$ — the minus sign is *critical*. A common student error is computing $\det(\lambda I - A)$ and forgetting this differs by the sign factor $(-1)^n$. Both forms are valid; just be **consistent** with your chosen convention.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Eigenvalue Problem

- **Population Dynamics (Leslie / Lefkovitch Models):** Dominant eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$ predicts the long-term growth rate of an age-structured population. If $\lambda_{\max} > 1$, the population grows; if $\lambda_{\max} < 1$, it dies out.
- **Pharmacokinetics:** The eigenvalues of the compartmental matrix determine the **elimination half-lives** of a drug from body compartments.
- **Vibration Analysis in Biomedical Equipment:** Natural frequencies of a beating-heart assist device correspond to eigenvalues of the mass-stiffness matrix.
- **Genomic Data (PCA):** Eigenvalues of the covariance matrix of gene-expression data indicate the **variance captured** by each principal component.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Eigenvalue Definition to Characteristic Equation

**Starting point:** By definition, $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of $A$ if and only if there exists $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ with $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$.

**Step 1.** Subtract the right side:
$$A\mathbf{v} - \lambda \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2.** Factor out the common vector $\mathbf{v}$ on the left by inserting the identity matrix $I$:
$$A\mathbf{v} - \lambda I \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3.** Combine into a single matrix-vector product:
$$(A - \lambda I)\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4.** This is a **homogeneous linear system** in $\mathbf{v}$. By the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra (rank-nullity), it has a non-trivial solution $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ **if and only if** the coefficient matrix is singular:
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

**Step 5.** Expanding the determinant produces a polynomial $p(\lambda)$ of degree $n$. The roots of $p(\lambda) = 0$ are precisely the eigenvalues of $A$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: $2 \times 2$ Case

Let
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}, \quad I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Then:
$$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

**Computing the determinant** (along the first row, cofactor expansion):
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - (b)(c)$$

**Expanding the product term-by-term:**
$$\begin{aligned}
(a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) &= a d - a \lambda - d \lambda + \lambda^2 \\
&= \lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + ad
\end{aligned}$$

**Combining with the cross term:**
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + ad - bc$$

**Recognizing $a+d = \operatorname{tr}(A)$ and $ad - bc = \det(A)$:**
$$\boxed{\;p(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - \operatorname{tr}(A)\,\lambda + \det(A) = 0\;}$$

**Verifying Vieta's formulas:**
- Sum of roots: $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = \operatorname{tr}(A) = a+d$ ✓
- Product of roots: $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 = \det(A) = ad - bc$ ✓

## 3.3 Worked Derivation: $3 \times 3$ Case (General Pattern)

For a $3 \times 3$ matrix $A$ with trace $\tau = \operatorname{tr}(A)$ and determinant $\Delta = \det(A)$, the characteristic polynomial is:
$$p(\lambda) = -\lambda^3 + \tau \lambda^2 - s_2 \lambda + \Delta$$
where $s_2$ is the sum of all $2 \times 2$ principal minors of $A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** For a $3\times 3$ matrix, students often mis-compute $s_2$. Remember, $s_2$ is the sum of the three principal $2 \times 2$ minors (delete one row AND the same column each time), **not** the sum of all $2\times 2$ sub-determinants.

## 3.4 Algorithmic Procedure for Finding Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors

> [!NOTE]
> The following five-step procedure is what the KTU board expects you to write out in full marks.

**STEP 1 — Form $A - \lambda I$:** Replace the diagonal entries of $A$ with $(a_{ii} - \lambda)$.

**STEP 2 — Compute the characteristic determinant:** Use cofactor expansion, row-reduction, or Sarrus' rule (for $3\times 3$) to expand $\det(A - \lambda I)$.

**STEP 3 — Set the determinant to zero and solve for $\lambda$:** This yields up to $n$ eigenvalues (counted with algebraic multiplicity). Complex roots come in **conjugate pairs** for real matrices.

**STEP 4 — For each distinct eigenvalue $\lambda_i$, solve $(A - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:** Row-reduce $(A - \lambda_i I)$ to echelon form. The free variables give parametric forms of the eigenvectors.

**STEP 5 — Verify normalization (optional but KTU-recommended):** Either leave eigenvectors unnormalized or scale them to unit length $\vert \mathbf{v} \vert = 1$. For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, the **cross-product verification** $A\mathbf{v} \stackrel{?}{=} \lambda \mathbf{v}$ is a fast sanity check.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full Walk-Through)

**Problem:** Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1.** Form $A - \lambda I$:
$$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2.** Characteristic determinant:
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - (1)(2)$$

**Step 3.** Expand and simplify:
$$\begin{aligned}
(4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 2 &= 12 - 4\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^2 - 2 \\
&= \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Set equal to zero and factor:
$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0$$
$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 5, \quad \lambda_2 = 2}$$

**Sanity check via trace-determinant identities:**
- $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 5 + 2 = 7 = \operatorname{tr}(A) = 4+3$ ✓
- $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 = 5 \times 2 = 10 = \det(A) = (4)(3) - (1)(2) = 10$ ✓

**Step 5.** Find eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 5$:
$$(A - 5I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row-reduce: $R_2 \to R_2 + 2R_1$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; -v_1 + v_2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; v_2 = v_1$$

Let $v_1 = s$ (free parameter). Then:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{v}_1 = s \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}}$$

Choosing $s = 1$ for simplicity: $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\; 1)^T$.

**Verification:** $A\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} = 5\mathbf{v}_1$ ✓

**Step 6.** Find eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$:
$$(A - 2I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row-reduce: $R_2 \to R_2 - R_1$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; 2v_1 + v_2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; v_2 = -2v_1$$

Let $v_1 = s$. Then:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{v}_2 = s \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}}$$

Choosing $s = 1$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1,\; -2)^T$.

**Verification:** $A\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} = 2\mathbf{v}_2$ ✓

**Final Result:** $\lambda_1 = 5, \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,1)^T$ and $\lambda_2 = 2, \mathbf{v}_2 = (1,-2)^T$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Connection to the Linear System Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Critical Link:** Once eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A$ are known, the constant-coefficient homogeneous linear system $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\mathbf{X}$ has the general solution:
> $$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\,\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\,\mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_n e^{\lambda_n t}\,\mathbf{v}_n$$
> This is precisely the *bridge* between the Fundamental Theorem (which guarantees a unique solution) and the Eigenvalue Problem (which **constructs** that solution in closed form).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — The Eigenvalue-Eigenvector Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Square Matrix A of order n]) --> B0[Form A minus lambda I]
    B0 --> C0[Compute det of A minus lambda I]
    C0 --> D0[Set determinant equal to zero]
    D0 --> E0[Characteristic Polynomial p of lambda of degree n]
    E0 --> F0[Solve p of lambda equals zero]
    F0 --> G0[Eigenvalues lambda 1, lambda 2 ... lambda n]
    G0 --> H0[For each lambda i form A minus lambda i I]
    H0 --> I0[Row reduce to echelon form]
    I0 --> J0[Identify free variables]
    J0 --> K0[Write parametric eigenvector v i]
    K0 --> L0[Verify A v i equals lambda i v i]
    L0 --> M0([Eigenvector Set Complete])
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Fundamental Theorem Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP1([Initial Condition X of t0 equals X0]) --> CON1{Is A of t and F of t continuous on I}
    CON1 -- Yes --> EXT[Existence Guaranteed on I]
    CON1 -- Yes --> UNI[Uniqueness Guaranteed on I]
    CON1 -- No --> FAIL[No Guarantee of Solution]
    EXT --> HOM{Homogeneous or Non-Homogeneous}
    UNI --> HOM
    HOM -- Homogeneous --> SOL1[General Solution Phi of t times C]
    HOM -- Non-Homogeneous --> SOL2[Complementary plus Particular Integral]
    SOL1 --> OUT1([Unique Solution on I])
    SOL2 --> OUT1
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Diagonalization via Eigen-Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0([Matrix A n by n]) --> Q1{Does A have n linearly independent eigenvectors}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Build P with columns v1 to vn]
    P1 --> P2[Build D diag lambda1 to lambda n]
    P2 --> P3[Compute P inverse]
    P3 --> P4[Form A equals P D P inverse]
    P4 --> SYS0[Solve dX over dt equals A X using X equals P Y]
    SYS0 --> DEC[Decoupled System dY over dt equals D Y]
    DEC --> SOL[Yi of t equals ci exp of lambda i t]
    SOL --> BACK[Back-substitute X equals P Y]
    BACK --> OUT0([Closed-Form Solution X of t])
    Q1 -- No --> ND[A is not diagonalizable use Jordan form]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Algebraic vs. Geometric Multiplicity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RT([Root lambda of multiplicity m]) --> CHK{Compute nullity of A minus lambda I}
    CHK --> N1[nullity equals m]
    CHK --> N2[nullity less than m]
    N1 --> DIAG[lambda is semisimple - diagonalizable contribution]
    N2 --> JORD[Need generalized eigenvectors - Jordan block of size greater than one]
    DIAG --> COUNT[Count linearly independent eigenvectors across all lambdas]
    JORD --> GEN[Construct chains from A minus lambda I power k times v equals zero]
    GEN --> COUNT
    COUNT --> FINAL{Total independent eigenvectors equals n}
    FINAL -- Yes --> DONE([A is diagonalizable])
    FINAL -- No --> PARTIAL([A is not diagonalizable in standard basis])
```

## 4.5 Concept-Map Linking Theorem to Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT([Fundamental Theorem of Linear Systems]) --> GUAR[Existence and Uniqueness on I]
    GUAR --> FORM[Existence of closed form]
    FORM --> EVP[Matrix Eigenvalue Problem]
    EVP --> CHAR[Characteristic Equation]
    CHAR --> LAMBDA[Eigenvalues lambda i]
    LAMBDA --> VEC[Eigenvectors v i]
    VEC --> GENSOL[General Solution c1 exp lambda1 t v1 plus ...]
    GENSOL --> IC[Apply Initial Condition to find constants]
    IC --> FINAL([Unique Trajectory X of t])
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]
**State the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Systems for a first-order initial value problem.**

**Model Answer:**
> Let $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t)$ with $\mathbf{X}(t_0) = \mathbf{X}_0$. If all entries of $A(t)$ and $\mathbf{F}(t)$ are **continuous on an open interval $I$ containing $t_0$**, then the IVP has a **unique solution** defined on the **entire interval $I$**. This solution is denoted by $\mathbf{X}(t)$ and satisfies the differential equation and the initial condition simultaneously. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]
**Define eigenvalue and eigenvector of an $n \times n$ matrix $A$.**

**Model Answer:**
> A scalar $\lambda$ is called an **eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a non-zero column vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (or $\mathbb{C}^n$) such that $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$. The non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ satisfying this equation is called an **eigenvector** of $A$ corresponding to the eigenvalue $\lambda$. To find $\lambda$, one solves the characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. *(3 marks)*
