## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]

**(a)** *State the Fundamental Theorem for linear systems. Discuss the existence and uniqueness of solutions for the homogeneous system $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\mathbf{X}$ with $\mathbf{X}(t_0) = \mathbf{0}$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, find the eigenvalues and the corresponding eigenvectors. Hence write the general solution of $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\mathbf{X}$.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement:** If $A(t)$ is a continuous $n \times n$ matrix on an open interval $I$ containing $t_0$, then the homogeneous initial value problem

$$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A(t)\,\mathbf{X}, \quad \mathbf{X}(t_0) = \mathbf{0}$$

has exactly one solution on $I$, namely $\mathbf{X}(t) \equiv \mathbf{0}$.

**Existence:** The zero function trivially satisfies both the differential equation and the initial condition, so existence is immediate. **(2 marks)**

**Uniqueness:** Suppose there were two distinct solutions $\mathbf{X}_1(t)$ and $\mathbf{X}_2(t)$ with $\mathbf{X}_1(t_0) = \mathbf{X}_2(t_0) = \mathbf{0}$. Let $\mathbf{Y}(t) = \mathbf{X}_1(t) - \mathbf{X}_2(t)$. By linearity, $\mathbf{Y}$ also satisfies the homogeneous equation with $\mathbf{Y}(t_0) = \mathbf{0}$. The Fundamental Theorem forces $\mathbf{Y} \equiv \mathbf{0}$, hence $\mathbf{X}_1 = \mathbf{X}_2$. **(3 marks)**

**Conclusion:** The only solution starting from the origin is the trivial one. For non-zero initial conditions, the homogeneous system admits a unique trajectory through every other starting point. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{pmatrix} 5-\lambda & 4 \\ 1 & 2-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (5-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 4 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**
$$\begin{aligned}
(5-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 4 &= 10 - 5\lambda - 2\lambda + \lambda^2 - 4 \\
&= \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve:** $(\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 1) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_1 = 6,\; \lambda_2 = 1$. **(1 mark)**

**Sanity check:** Sum $= 6+1 = 7 = \operatorname{tr}(A) = 5+2$ ✓, Product $= 6 = \det(A) = 10 - 4$ ✓. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 6$:**
$$(A - 6I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{0}$$

Row-reduce: $R_2 \to R_2 + R_1 \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, giving $-v_1 + 4v_2 = 0$, i.e., $v_1 = 4v_2$. Choose $v_2 = 1$:
$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 1$:**
$$(A - I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{0}$$

Row-reduce: $R_2 \to R_2 - \tfrac{1}{4}R_1 \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, giving $v_1 + v_2 = 0$, i.e., $v_1 = -v_2$. Choose $v_2 = 1$:
$$\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 6 — General solution of $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\mathbf{X}$:**

Since $A\mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$, the functions $e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$ are linearly independent solutions. Hence:
$$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 e^{6t}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(2 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retest Pattern]

**(a)** *For the system $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t)$ where $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{F}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 3e^{t} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A$, and hence solve the non-homogeneous system.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the difference between algebraic and geometric multiplicity. Under what conditions is a matrix diagonalizable? Illustrate with an example where the algebraic multiplicity is 2 but the geometric multiplicity is 1.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation of $A$:**
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^2 - 1 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (2-\lambda)^2 = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; 2 - \lambda = \pm 1$$
$$\lambda_1 = 1, \quad \lambda_2 = 3$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 1$:**
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; v_1 + v_2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 3$:**
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; -v_1 + v_2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Complementary function:**
$$\mathbf{X}_c(t) = c_1 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{3t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Particular integral by undetermined coefficients:** Try $\mathbf{X}_p = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix} e^{t}$.
Substituting:
$$\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix} e^{t} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix} e^{t} + \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} e^{t}$$

Equating coefficients of $e^{t}$:
$$\begin{cases} a = 2a + b + 3 \\ b = a + 2b \end{cases} \;\Longrightarrow\; \begin{cases} -a - b = 3 \\ -a + b = 0 \end{cases} \;\Longrightarrow\; a = -\tfrac{3}{2},\; b = -\tfrac{3}{2}$$

So $\mathbf{X}_p = -\tfrac{3}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} e^{t}$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 6 — General solution:**
$$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{3t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \frac{3}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} e^{t}$$ **(1 mark — can be merged into the form of the answer)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Resonant Forcing:** Because the forcing function $e^{t}$ matches the homogeneous eigenvalue $\lambda_1 = 1$, the trial solution $\mathbf{X}_p = \mathbf{a} e^{t}$ is **resonant** — and the augmented system still gives a valid particular integral (since the non-resonant component $\mathbf{v}_2$ is available to absorb the resonance, because $1$ is a simple root of the characteristic polynomial). If $\lambda_1$ had been a repeated root, the trial would need to be $\mathbf{X}_p = t(\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} t + \cdots)e^{t}$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definition — Algebraic Multiplicity:** The algebraic multiplicity $\operatorname{am}(\lambda)$ is the multiplicity of $\lambda$ as a root of the characteristic polynomial $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Definition — Geometric Multiplicity:** The geometric multiplicity $\operatorname{gm}(\lambda)$ is the dimension of the eigenspace $\ker(A - \lambda I)$, i.e., the number of linearly independent eigenvectors corresponding to $\lambda$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Relation:** For every eigenvalue, $1 \le \operatorname{gm}(\lambda) \le \operatorname{am}(\lambda)$. **(1 mark)**

**Diagonalizability Theorem:** An $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is diagonalizable if and only if for every eigenvalue, $\operatorname{gm}(\lambda) = \operatorname{am}(\lambda)$ — equivalently, $A$ has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors. **(1.5 marks)**

**Illustrative Counter-Example:** Consider
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Characteristic polynomial:** $(1-\lambda)^2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda = 1$ with $\operatorname{am}(1) = 2$. **(0.5 marks)**

**Eigenspace:** $(A - I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; v_2 = 0$, $v_1$ free. The eigenspace is spanned by $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, so $\operatorname{gm}(1) = 1 \neq 2 = \operatorname{am}(1)$. **(1 mark)**

**Conclusion:** $A$ is **not diagonalizable** since we cannot find 2 linearly independent eigenvectors. A Jordan block of size 2 is required. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign Error in Characteristic Equation (2 marks lost):** Computing $\det(\lambda I - A)$ and forgetting the sign convention when stating eigenvalues. **Always write the equation, then state the convention you used.**
> 2. **Zero Vector Mistake (1 mark):** Stating $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ as an eigenvector. By definition, eigenvectors must be **non-zero**.
> 3. **Missing Verification Step (1 mark):** Not substituting back to confirm $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$. Examiners *love* this quick check; skipping it signals weak understanding.
> 4. **Confusing Algebraic and Geometric Multiplicity (3 marks):** Treating them as the same number. They are equal **only** in well-behaved cases; for defective matrices they differ.
> 5. **Forgetting Initial Conditions in the General Solution (2 marks):** The General Solution has arbitrary constants; the **Particular Solution** has them evaluated. State both clearly.
> 6. **No statement of continuity assumption (2 marks in Q-A):** The Fundamental Theorem's continuity hypothesis is **part of the theorem** — not an aside. Always state it.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM** = Existence + Uniqueness + Continuity hypothesis on $I \ni t_0$.
- [x] **Homogeneous** system: forcing $\mathbf{F}(t) = \mathbf{0}$; non-homogeneous has $\mathbf{F}(t) \neq \mathbf{0}$.
- [x] **Trivial solution** $\mathbf{X} \equiv \mathbf{0}$ exists for every homogeneous system with $\mathbf{X}(t_0) = \mathbf{0}$; the Fundamental Theorem guarantees it is the **only** one.
- [x] **General solution of non-homogeneous** = Complementary function + Particular integral (superposition principle).
- [x] **Eigenvalue equation:** $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$, with $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$.
- [x] **Characteristic equation:** $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ — polynomial of degree $n$.
- [x] **Eigenvector procedure:** Solve $(A - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ for each $\lambda_i$.
- [x] **Trace identity:** $\sum \lambda_i = \operatorname{tr}(A)$.
- [x] **Determinant identity:** $\prod \lambda_i = \det(A)$.
- [x] **Algebraic multiplicity** ≥ **Geometric multiplicity** always.
- [x] **Diagonalizability condition:** Geometric = Algebraic multiplicity for **every** eigenvalue.
- [x] **Bridge to ODEs:** $\mathbf{X}(t) = \sum c_i e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$ for constant-coefficient systems.
- [x] **Complex eigenvalues** of a real matrix come in conjugate pairs $\alpha \pm i\beta$, giving real solutions $e^{\alpha t}\cos(\beta t)$ and $e^{\alpha t}\sin(\beta t)$ via the real and imaginary parts of the complex eigenvector.
- [x] **Sanity check before submission:** Always verify $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$ and use trace/det identities to catch arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Calculus - Differentiation from First Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE -1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Calculus - Differentiation from First Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Differentiation from First Principles** (also called the **ab initio** or **limit definition of the derivative**) is the rigorous mathematical procedure of determining the *instantaneous rate of change* of a function with respect to its independent variable, derived directly from the fundamental concept of a **limit**, without recourse to pre-established differentiation rules.

Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on an open interval. The **derivative of $f$ at a point $x$**, denoted $f'(x)$, is formally defined as:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$

provided this limit exists and is finite. The function is said to be **differentiable** at $x$ if and only if this two-sided limit is well-defined. This definition can equivalently be expressed using the increment $h$ replaced by an alternative variable $\Delta x$ (the change in $x$):

$$f'(x) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)}{\Delta x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Differentiation from first principles is the *foundational pillar* of the differential calculus unit. Mastery of this concept is mandatory before progressing to standard rules (Product, Quotient, Chain) and their applications in life sciences — particularly in **growth models**, **pharmacokinetics**, and **neural signaling rates**.

The derivative has a powerful **geometric interpretation**: it is precisely the **gradient (slope) of the tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $(x, f(x))$. The expression $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ represents the *gradient of the secant line* joining two points on the curve, and taking the limit as $h \to 0$ "squeezes" this secant line into the tangent.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car along a highway, and your **odometer** records the total distance traveled. Your *average speed* over any time interval is simply:

$$\text{Average Speed} = \frac{\text{Total Distance Change}}{\text{Total Time Elapsed}}$$

This is exactly analogous to the **difference quotient** $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ — it tells you the average rate of change between two points.

Now, your car's **speedometer** displays the *instantaneous* speed at any exact moment. The speedometer is the physical embodiment of differentiation from first principles! It works by calculating the average speed over an extremely tiny time window (approaching zero) and displaying that as the current speed. Mathematically, the **smaller the time interval**, the **closer the average speed gets to the true instantaneous speed**.

| Real-World Object | Mathematical Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Speedometer reading | $f'(x)$ — the derivative |
| Car's position function $s(t)$ | Function $f(x)$ |
| Small time interval $\Delta t$ | Increment $h$ |
| Average speed over a journey | $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ |
| Exact instantaneous speed | $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Life Science Connection:** In biology, the derivative of a *population function* $P(t)$ with respect to time, $P'(t)$, represents the *instantaneous growth rate* of a population at time $t$. Biologists use this to determine whether a species population is growing, declining, or stable at any specific moment — a critical concept in ecological modeling.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Notation

- **$f'(x)$** — *Prime notation* (Lagrange's notation), the most common form in KTU examinations.
- **$\dfrac{dy}{dx}$** — *Leibniz notation*, where $y = f(x)$. It is read as "*dee-y by dee-x*".
- **$\dfrac{d}{dx} f(x)$** — *Operator notation*, treating differentiation as an operation applied to $f(x)$.
- **$\dot{y}$** — *Newton's dot notation*, used primarily when the independent variable is **time** (e.g., $\dot{x}(t)$ for velocity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is **NOT** a fraction in the algebraic sense — it is a single indivisible operator. However, in many physical applications (especially in life sciences), it *behaves* like a fraction, which is why Leibniz's notation is so useful in differential equations and modeling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Architecture of Differentiation

The process of differentiation from first principles follows a strict, four-stage logical pipeline. Every KTU board exam problem will demand that you execute these stages in order.

### Stage 1: Function Identification
Identify the function $f(x)$ whose derivative is required. The function must be **continuous** at the point of differentiation (a necessary condition, though not sufficient for differentiability).

### Stage 2: Substitution Step
Compute $f(x+h)$ by replacing **every occurrence of $x$** in the function's expression with the binomial $(x+h)$. This is the most error-prone step for KTU students — a single missed substitution propagates throughout the solution.

### Stage 3: Algebraic Simplification
Form the difference quotient $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ and simplify the numerator using:
- **Binomial expansion:** $(x+h)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} h^k$
- **Factoring techniques:** Especially extracting a common factor of $h$
- **Trigonometric identities:** Such as $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$

### Stage 4: Application of the Limit
Substitute $h = 0$ into the simplified expression. If the result is **finite and well-defined**, the derivative exists. If it yields an indeterminate form $\frac{0}{0}$ or $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$, the function is **not differentiable** at that point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Four Stages Matter in Life Sciences:** The substitution $x \to x+h$ mathematically models a "*small perturbation*" — the fundamental concept behind how living systems respond to tiny changes. Pharmacologists use this exact framework to calculate **drug sensitivity** ($dD/dC$, the rate of change of drug effect with concentration).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains the core derivative results that you must commit to memory. Every formula listed here is derivable from first principles, and KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive one of these.

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Domain Restrictions | Life Science Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $c$ (constant) | $0$ | All $\mathbb{R}$ | Zero growth rate in equilibrium models |
| $x^n$ ($n \in \mathbb{R}$) | $n x^{n-1}$ | Varies with $n$ | Allometric scaling laws in biology |
| $e^x$ | $e^x$ | All $\mathbb{R}$ | Exponential bacterial growth |
| $a^x$ ($a > 0$) | $a^x \ln(a)$ | All $\mathbb{R}$ | Radioactive decay modeling |
| $\ln(x)$ | $\dfrac{1}{x}$ | $x > 0$ | Logarithmic growth (rare species) |
| $\sin(x)$ | $\cos(x)$ | All $\mathbb{R}$ | Oscillatory neural signals |
| $\cos(x)$ | $-\sin(x)$ | All $\mathbb{R}$ | AC bioelectric current analysis |
| $\tan(x)$ | $\sec^2(x)$ | $x \neq \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$ | Slope of reaction rate curves |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Note (Table Safety):** Notice the use of `\dfrac{1}{x}` instead of `\frac{1}{x}` or the vertical pipe symbol `\vert`. KTU answer sheets require clean notation — **never** write $\frac{1}{x}$ ambiguously; always use `\dfrac` in display contexts for examiner clarity.

## 2.3 Engineering & Life Science Utility of Differentiation

The derivative is not merely a mathematical curiosity — it is the **fundamental language of change** used across disciplines:

- **Pharmacokinetics:** The rate of drug absorption $C'(t)$ determines dosing intervals.
- **Ecology:** Population dynamics $P'(t)$ predicts species survival under environmental stress.
- **Epidemiology:** The derivative of the infection curve identifies the **peak infection rate** (the inflection point).
- **Genetics:** Mutation rate is the derivative of allele frequency with respect to generation number.
- **Biochemistry:** Enzyme reaction rates are derivatives of substrate concentration with respect to time (Michaelis-Menten kinetics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind First Principles:** Why do engineers and life scientists bother with the cumbersome first-principles method when shortcut rules exist? Because **first principles reveal the underlying structure** of the derivative. When a function is exotic (e.g., a custom population model with no standard rule), the first-principles method is the **only** universal tool. KTU values this deep understanding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Power Function $f(x) = x^2$

This is the **single most frequently asked derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme Module 2 examinations. We will execute it with full rigor.

**Step 1: State the Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$

This is the **starting axiom** — we are not assuming any prior rule.

**Step 2: Compute $f(x+h)$ by Direct Substitution**

$$f(x+h) = (x+h)^2$$

Expand the binomial using the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$f(x+h) = x^2 + 2xh + h^2$$

**Step 3: Form the Difference Quotient**

Substitute into the definition:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\left(x^2 + 2xh + h^2\right) - x^2}{h}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the Numerator**

The $x^2$ terms cancel exactly:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{2xh + h^2}{h}$$

**Step 5: Factor out the Common Factor $h$**

This is the **critical algebraic step** that enables the limit to be evaluated:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h(2x + h)}{h}$$

**Step 6: Cancel the Common Factor**

Provided $h \neq 0$ (which is guaranteed in the limit process since $h$ approaches but never equals zero):

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} (2x + h)$$

**Step 7: Apply the Limit**

Since $2x$ is constant with respect to $h$, and $h \to 0$:

$$f'(x) = 2x + 0 = 2x$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{f'(x) = 2x}$$

This matches the power rule: $\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}$ with $n=2$ giving $2x^1 = 2x$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The General Power Rule $f(x) = x^n$ (Positive Integer)

This derivation generalizes the previous result using the **Binomial Theorem**.

**Step 1: Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h)^n - x^n}{h}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Binomial Theorem**

For positive integer $n$:

$$(x+h)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} h^k = x^n + n x^{n-1} h + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h^2 + \cdots + h^n$$

**Step 3: Subtract $x^n$ from the Expansion**

The $k=0$ term is $x^n$, which cancels:

$$(x+h)^n - x^n = n x^{n-1} h + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h^2 + \cdots + h^n$$

**Step 4: Factor out $h$**

Every term in the remaining expansion contains at least one factor of $h$:

$$(x+h)^n - x^n = h \left[ n x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h + \cdots + h^{n-1} \right]$$

**Step 5: Form the Difference Quotient and Cancel $h$**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \left[ n x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h + \cdots + h^{n-1} \right]$$

**Step 6: Apply the Limit**

All terms containing $h$ vanish as $h \to 0$. The only surviving term is the first:

$$f'(x) = n x^{n-1}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}\left(x^n\right) = n x^{n-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Validity:** Although we derived this for positive integer $n$, the result holds for *all real* $n \neq 0$. The proof for arbitrary real exponents requires the Generalized Binomial Theorem and logarithmic differentiation — beyond the KTU 2024 Module 2 scope, but worth noting for conceptual completeness.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivative of $f(x) = \sin(x)$

This derivation uses the **Squeeze Theorem** and two critical trigonometric limits.

**Prerequisite Limits (Must be memorized):**

$$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(h)}{h} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(h) - 1}{h} = 0$$

**Step 1: Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(x+h) - \sin(x)}{h}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Sine Addition Formula**

$$\sin(x+h) = \sin(x)\cos(h) + \cos(x)\sin(h)$$

**Step 3: Substitute and Group**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(x)\cos(h) + \cos(x)\sin(h) - \sin(x)}{h}$$

Rearrange to isolate the common factor $\sin(x)$:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(x)[\cos(h) - 1] + \cos(x)\sin(h)}{h}$$

**Step 4: Split into Two Limits**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \sin(x) \cdot \frac{\cos(h) - 1}{h} + \lim_{h \to 0} \cos(x) \cdot \frac{\sin(h)}{h}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate Each Limit**

Since $\sin(x)$ and $\cos(x)$ are constants with respect to $h$:

$$f'(x) = \sin(x) \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(h) - 1}{h} + \cos(x) \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(h)}{h}$$

Applying the prerequisite limits:

$$f'(x) = \sin(x) \cdot (0) + \cos(x) \cdot (1)$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}[\sin(x)] = \cos(x)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Derivative of $f(x) = e^x$

The exponential function has a **unique and beautiful** first-principles derivation.

**Step 1: Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{x+h} - e^x}{h}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Exponent Law**

Since $e^{x+h} = e^x \cdot e^h$:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^x \cdot e^h - e^x}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^x(e^h - 1)}{h}$$

**Step 3: Factor Out the Constant $e^x$**

$$f'(x) = e^x \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^h - 1}{h}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Fundamental Exponential Limit**

The limit $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^h - 1}{h} = 1$ is one of the foundational limits of calculus, derivable from the series expansion $e^h = 1 + h + \frac{h^2}{2!} + \cdots$.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}\left[e^x\right] = e^x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Life Sciences:** The fact that $e^x$ is its own derivative is precisely why exponential growth is the natural model for **unrestricted bacterial reproduction**, **viral spread in a susceptible population**, and **compound interest in biological resource allocation**. The exponential function is the only function (up to scaling) that preserves its shape under differentiation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Sub-technique | Typical Mark Allocation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polynomial $x^n$ | Binomial Theorem | 3–7 marks |
| Trigonometric $\sin/\cos$ | Addition formulas + Squeeze | 7–14 marks |
| Exponential $e^x$ | Exponent laws + fundamental limit | 5–7 marks |
| Composite expression | Nested substitution | 7 marks |
| Piecewise function | Left/right limit verification | 14 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Interpretation: Secant to Tangent

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the limit process that transforms a secant line into a tangent line — the geometric heart of differentiation from first principles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two distinct points P and Q on curve y equals f of x] --> B[Form secant line PQ with gradient m sub h]
    B --> C[Compute difference quotient: f of x plus h minus f of x all divided by h]
    C --> D[Reduce the increment h: h becomes 0.5]
    D --> E[Point Q approaches Point P along the curve]
    E --> F[Repeat: h becomes 0.1]
    F --> G[Repeat: h becomes 0.01]
    G --> H[Repeat: h becomes 0.001]
    H --> I[Apply limit as h approaches zero]
    I --> J[Secant line PQ rotates and converges to a single line]
    J --> K[Final Result: Tangent line at Point P with gradient f prime of x]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:3px
    style I fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram traces the evolution of the secant line as the increment $h$ shrinks. At each stage, the secant line "rotates" around the fixed point $P$, and in the limit, it locks into the unique tangent position. This is the geometric intuition behind the entire first-principles method.

## 4.2 Algorithmic Flowchart: Executing a First-Principles Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Receive function f of x to differentiate] --> Step1[Step 1: Compute f of x plus h by substitution]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Form the difference quotient numerator]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Subtract f of x from the numerator]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Factor out h from the numerator]
    Step4 --> Check{Does h factor out cleanly?}
    Check -->|Yes| Step5[Step 5: Cancel h from numerator and denominator]
    Check -->|No| Step6[Step 5 Alt: Apply limit theorems directly or use known limits]
    Step5 --> Step7[Step 6: Substitute h equals 0]
    Step6 --> Step7
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 7: State the final derivative f prime of x]
    Step8 --> End[Write boxed final answer]
    
    style Start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style Check fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style End fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Connecting Differentiation to Life Science Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Calculus[Differentiation from First Principles] --> A1[Geometric Meaning: Tangent Slope]
    Calculus --> A2[Physical Meaning: Instantaneous Rate]
    Calculus --> A3[Algebraic Meaning: Linear Approximation]
    
    A1 --> B1[Curve Sketching in Biology]
    A2 --> B2[Population Growth Rate P prime of t]
    A2 --> B3[Drug Concentration Change dC over dt]
    A2 --> B4[Reaction Velocity v equals dS over dt]
    A3 --> B5[Taylor Series Approximations]
    A3 --> B6[Sensitivity Analysis in Models]
    
    B1 --> C1[KTU Exam Application]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C2[Higher Semester Module]
    B6 --> C2
    
    style Calculus fill:#0066cc,stroke:#003d7a,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#28a745,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#fff
    style C2 fill:#6c757d,stroke:#495057,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the derivative of a function $f(x)$ from first principles. Hence compute $f'(2)$ for $f(x) = 3x^2 - 5x + 7$.**

**Model Answer:**

The derivative of $f(x)$ at a point $x$ is defined as:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$

For $f(x) = 3x^2 - 5x + 7$, using the standard power rule (which itself is derived from first principles):

$$f'(x) = 6x - 5$$

Therefore:

$$f'(2) = 6(2) - 5 = 12 - 5 = 7$$

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the geometric significance of the derivative. If the derivative of a population function $P(t)$ at $t=5$ equals $12$, interpret this result biologically.**

**Model Answer:**

The **geometric significance** of $f'(x)$ is that it equals the **slope (gradient) of the tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $(x, f(x))$.

The **biological interpretation** of $P'(5) = 12$ is that at time $t = 5$ (units), the population is increasing at the instantaneous rate of **12 individuals per unit time**. This means that *at that exact moment*, if the growth conditions remained momentarily constant, the population would be gaining 12 members per unit time interval.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark problems, KTU examiners allocate marks based on **process**, not just the final answer. You MUST show: (1) the general definition, (2) the substitution step, (3) the algebraic simplification with explicit factor extraction, (4) the limit application, and (5) the boxed final answer. Skipping any stage costs 2–3 marks immediately. Do NOT write the final answer without showing the four-stage pipeline.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the derivative of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x$ from first principles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using first principles, prove that the derivative of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is $f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ for $x > 0$.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Write the First-Principles Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$

**Step 2: Compute $f(x+h)$**

$$f(x+h) = (x+h)^3 - 2(x+h)$$

Expanding $(x+h)^3$ using the binomial theorem:

$$(x+h)^3 = x^3 + 3x^2 h + 3xh^2 + h^3$$

Therefore:

$$f(x+h) = x^3 + 3x^2 h + 3xh^2 + h^3 - 2x - 2h$$

**Step 3: Form the Difference Quotient**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\left(x^3 + 3x^2 h + 3xh^2 + h^3 - 2x - 2h\right) - \left(x^3 - 2x\right)}{h}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the Numerator**

The $x^3$ terms cancel. The $-2x$ terms also cancel:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{3x^2 h + 3xh^2 + h^3 - 2h}{h}$$

**Step 5: Factor Out $h$**

Every term in the numerator contains $h$:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h\left(3x^2 + 3xh + h^2 - 2\right)}{h}$$

**Step 6: Cancel and Apply the Limit**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \left(3x^2 + 3xh + h^2 - 2\right)$$

Substituting $h = 0$:

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 + 0 + 0 - 2 = 3x^2 - 2$$

$$\boxed{f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Write the Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{x+h} - \sqrt{x}}{h}$$

**Step 2: Recognize the Indeterminate Form**

Direct substitution of $h=0$ gives $\frac{0}{0}$. We must use **rationalization** to resolve this.

**Step 3: Multiply Numerator and Denominator by the Conjugate**

The conjugate of $\sqrt{x+h} - \sqrt{x}$ is $\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}$:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\left(\sqrt{x+h} - \sqrt{x}\right)\left(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\right)}{h\left(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\right)}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Difference of Squares Identity**

$$(a-b)(a+b) = a^2 - b^2$$

The numerator simplifies dramatically:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h) - x}{h\left(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\right)} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{h\left(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\right)}$$

**Step 5: Cancel $h$**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}}$$

**Step 6: Apply the Limit**

$$f'(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+0} + \sqrt{x}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$$

$$\boxed{f'(x) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Result Matters:** This confirms the power rule for $n = \frac{1}{2}$: $\frac{d}{dx}\left(x^{1/2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}x^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$. The rationalization technique demonstrated here is essential for differentiating all radical functions from first principles.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Internal Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ from first principles, where $x \neq 0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using first principles, find the derivative of $f(x) = \cos(x)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\frac{1}{x+h} - \frac{1}{x}}{h}$$

**Step 2: Combine the Fractions in the Numerator**

Common denominator $(x+h)x$:

$$\frac{1}{x+h} - \frac{1}{x} = \frac{x - (x+h)}{x(x+h)} = \frac{-h}{x(x+h)}$$

**Step 3: Form the Full Difference Quotient**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-h}{h \cdot x(x+h)}$$

**Step 4: Cancel $h$**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-1}{x(x+h)}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Limit**

$$f'(x) = \frac{-1}{x(x+0)} = -\frac{1}{x^2}$$

$$\boxed{f'(x) = -\frac{1}{x^2}}$$

This confirms the power rule for $n = -1$: $\frac{d}{dx}(x^{-1}) = -x^{-2} = -\frac{1}{x^2}$. ✓

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Definition**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(x+h) - \cos(x)}{h}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Cosine Addition Formula**

$$\cos(x+h) = \cos(x)\cos(h) - \sin(x)\sin(h)$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(x)\cos(h) - \sin(x)\sin(h) - \cos(x)}{h}$$

Group the $\cos(x)$ terms:

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(x)[\cos(h) - 1] - \sin(x)\sin(h)}{h}$$

**Step 4: Split the Limit**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \cos(x) \cdot \frac{\cos(h) - 1}{h} - \lim_{h \to 0} \sin(x) \cdot \frac{\sin(h)}{h}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Prerequisite Limits**

Using $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(h) - 1}{h} = 0$ and $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(h)}{h} = 1$:

$$f'(x) = \cos(x) \cdot (0) - \sin(x) \cdot (1) = -\sin(x)$$

$$\boxed{f'(x) = -\sin(x)}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examinations:**

- **The Golden Definition:** $f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ — must be written from memory at the start of every first-principles problem.

- **Four-Stage Pipeline:** (1) Substitute, (2) Form difference quotient, (3) Factor out $h$, (4) Apply limit $h \to 0$. Missing a stage = losing marks.

- **The $h$-Factor Rule:** After forming $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$, the numerator **must** contain a factor of $h$ for the derivative to exist. If it doesn't, the function is not differentiable at that point.

- **Trigonometric Prerequisite Limits:** Memorize $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(h)}{h} = 1$ and $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos(h) - 1}{h} = 0$ — they are the engines behind all trig derivatives.

- **Exponential Identity:** $e^x$ is the **only** function (up to a multiplicative constant) equal to its own derivative. This uniqueness is why exponential models dominate in life science growth equations.

- **Rationalization Technique:** For radicals ($\sqrt{x}$, $\sqrt[3]{x}$), always multiply by the conjugate before factoring out $h$.

- **Notation Mastery:** KTU examiners accept all four notations — $f'(x)$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}f(x)$, and $\dot{y}$ — but be consistent within a single answer.

- **Indeterminate Forms:** Encountering $\frac{0}{0}$ in the difference quotient is **normal and expected**. It signals that further algebraic manipulation is required — not a calculation error.

- **Existence of Derivative:** A function is differentiable at $x$ only if the **left-hand limit** ($\lim_{h \to 0^-}$) **equals** the **right-hand limit** ($\lim_{h \to 0^+}$). For piecewise functions, both must be verified separately.

- **Life Science Hooks:** Always relate the derivative to a physical/biological rate — population change, drug concentration, enzyme velocity. KTU life science papers reward contextual interpretation.

- **No Vertical Pipes in Tables:** When writing derivatives in tabular form, use `\dfrac{1}{x}` or `\frac{1}{x}` — **never** the bare pipe symbol $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the table renderer.

- **Final Answer Box:** Always conclude with a boxed result: $\boxed{f'(x) = \ldots}$ — examiners use this as a quick reference for partial marking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differentiation of Common Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE -1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differentiation of Common Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Derivative

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for life-science mathematics, the **derivative** of a real-valued function $f(x)$ with respect to $x$ is formally defined as the limit of the difference quotient as the increment $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$
f'(x) \;=\; \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)}{\Delta x} \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}
$$

This limit, when it exists, is also denoted $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}f(x)$, or $D_x f(x)$. **Geometrically**, the derivative is the **instantaneous slope of the tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $x$. **Physically**, it is the **instantaneous rate of change** of one quantity with respect to another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (GDMAT101 / Module 2):** Students must memorize the derivatives of all standard elementary functions (algebraic, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric, inverse-trigonometric, and hyperbolic) and apply them to model biological, pharmacological, and ecological scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car along a straight road. The **distance** travelled is a function of **time**, say $s(t)$. The **speedometer** in your dashboard does not measure the average speed over an hour — it tells you the **instantaneous rate** at which your distance is changing **right now**.

The derivative $s'(t)$ is mathematically the **speedometer reading** of the function $s(t)$:

- **Position $\to$ Velocity** : $\displaystyle v(t) = \frac{ds}{dt}$
- **Velocity $\to$ Acceleration** : $\displaystyle a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$

In the **life sciences**, the same logic applies:

| Life-Science Quantity | Variable | Derivative | Real Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bacterial population | $P(t)$ | $P'(t)$ | Growth rate of colony |
| Drug concentration in blood | $C(t)$ | $C'(t)$ | Rate of absorption/elimination |
| Radioactive decay | $N(t)$ | $N'(t)$ | Rate of decay (negatively signed) |
| Heart chamber volume | $V(t)$ | $V'(t)$ | Flow rate of blood |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Whenever a quantity is **changing**, differentiation tells you **how fast** it is changing and in which **direction** (increasing or decreasing). If the derivative is positive, life is "growing"; if negative, life is "shrinking"; if zero, it is at a steady state (maximum, minimum, or plateau).

## 1.3 The Family of Common (Elementary) Functions

In the GDMAT101 syllabus, **"common functions"** refers to the standard library of elementary functions whose derivatives are well-known and must be memorized. They fall into six families:

1. **Constant & Power Functions** — $c, \, x^{n}$
2. **Exponential Functions** — $e^{x}, \, a^{x}$
3. **Logarithmic Functions** — $\ln x, \, \log_{a} x$
4. **Trigonometric Functions** — $\sin x, \cos x, \tan x, \cot x, \sec x, \csc x$
5. **Inverse Trigonometric Functions** — $\sin^{-1}x, \cos^{-1}x, \tan^{-1}x, \ldots$
6. **Hyperbolic Functions** — $\sinh x, \cosh x, \tanh x$

The remainder of these notes is dedicated to deriving, tabulating, and applying the differentiation rules for each of these families.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Rules of Differentiation

Differentiation is governed by a small set of **algebraic rules** that allow us to differentiate complex expressions by breaking them into elementary components. These rules form the **operational grammar** of calculus.

### 2.1.1 Linearity (Sum & Difference Rule)

The derivative of a sum (or difference) is the sum (or difference) of the derivatives. Constants are preserved, and a scalar multiplier can be pulled outside the differential operator:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[c_1 f(x) + c_2 g(x)\bigr] \;=\; c_1 f'(x) + c_2 g'(x)
$$

**The "Why":** This follows directly from the limit laws of continuity. The limit distributes over finite sums.

### 2.1.2 Product Rule

When two functions are **multiplied**, the derivative is **not** simply the product of derivatives. Use the **Leibniz rule**:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[f(x)\,g(x)\bigr] \;=\; f'(x)\,g(x) + f(x)\,g'(x)
$$

**Mnemonic — "First times Derivative of Second plus Second times Derivative of First."**

### 2.1.3 Quotient Rule

For a ratio of two differentiable functions (with $g(x) \neq 0$):

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\right] \;=\; \frac{f'(x)\,g(x) - f(x)\,g'(x)}{\bigl[g(x)\bigr]^{2}}
$$

**Mnemonic — "Low D-High minus High D-Low, over Low-Low (squared)."**

### 2.1.4 Chain Rule (Composition Rule)

If $y = f(u)$ and $u = g(x)$, then $y = f(g(x))$ is a composite function. Its derivative is:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} \;=\; \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} \qquad \text{or} \qquad \frac{d}{dx}f(g(x)) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)
$$

**The "Why":** The "chain" multiplies the rates of change at each layer. In pharmacology, this is the principle of **sequential rate processes** (drug absorption $\to$ distribution $\to$ metabolism).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Differentiation Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULA TABLE FOR KTU EXAMINATIONS** — All entries below are examinable. Memorize the symbol, the rule, and at least one biological application.

### Table 2.A — Algebraic & Power Functions

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Biological / Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $c$ (constant) | $0$ | Steady-state equilibrium |
| $x$ | $1$ | Identity rate of change |
| $x^{n}$ | $n\,x^{n-1}$ | Allometric scaling laws in biology |
| $\dfrac{1}{x} = x^{-1}$ | $-\dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$ | Reciprocal growth (e.g., Michaelis constant $1/K_m$) |
| $\sqrt{x} = x^{1/2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ | Half-power decay |

### Table 2.B — Exponential & Logarithmic Functions

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Biological / Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $e^{x}$ | $e^{x}$ | Self-replicating populations, radioactive decay |
| $a^{x}$ | $a^{x} \ln a$ | General exponential growth/decay |
| $\ln x$ | $\dfrac{1}{x}$ | Allometric relations $y = a \ln(x) + b$ |
| $\log_{a} x$ | $\dfrac{1}{x \ln a}$ | pH scales, decibel scales |
| $e^{f(x)}$ | $e^{f(x)} \cdot f'(x)$ | Chain rule applied to exponential |
| $\ln f(x)$ | $\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$ | Logarithmic differentiation |

### Table 2.C — Trigonometric Functions

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Biological / Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sin x$ | $\cos x$ | Oscillatory behaviour in neuronal firing |
| $\cos x$ | $-\sin x$ | Periodic respiration, ECG waveforms |
| $\tan x$ | $\sec^{2} x$ | Slope of trigonometric curves |
| $\cot x$ | $-\csc^{2} x$ | Complementary oscillations |
| $\sec x$ | $\sec x \tan x$ | Optics and lens curvature |
| $\csc x$ | $-\csc x \cot x$ | Reciprocal trig applications |

### Table 2.D — Inverse Trigonometric Functions

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Domain Restriction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sin^{-1} x$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}}$ | $\vert x \vert < 1$ |
| $\cos^{-1} x$ | $-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}}$ | $\vert x \vert < 1$ |
| $\tan^{-1} x$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + x^{2}}$ | All $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\cot^{-1} x$ | $-\dfrac{1}{1 + x^{2}}$ | All $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\sec^{-1} x$ | $\dfrac{1}{\vert x \vert \sqrt{x^{2} - 1}}$ | $\vert x \vert > 1$ |
| $\csc^{-1} x$ | $-\dfrac{1}{\vert x \vert \sqrt{x^{2} - 1}}$ | $\vert x \vert > 1$ |

### Table 2.E — Hyperbolic Functions

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Biological / Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sinh x$ | $\cosh x$ | Catenary curves, suspension bridges |
| $\cosh x$ | $\sinh x$ | Same as above |
| $\tanh x$ | $\operatorname{sech}^{2} x$ | Sigmoid activation in neural networks |
| $\coth x$ | $-\operatorname{csch}^{2} x$ | Inverse hyperbolic relations |
| $\operatorname{sech} x$ | $-\operatorname{sech} x \tanh x$ | Soliton waves |
| $\operatorname{csch} x$ | $-\operatorname{csch} x \coth x$ | Singular hyperbolic decay |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Life Sciences

The seemingly abstract derivatives above model the **physical pulse of the natural world**:

- **Population Dynamics** — The derivative of the logistic growth function $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP\!\left(1 - \dfrac{P}{K}\right)$ dictates whether a species thrives, stabilizes, or dies out. The carrying capacity $K$ is found by setting $P'(t) = 0$.
- **Pharmacokinetics** — Drug plasma concentration $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ decays exponentially. The rate of elimination is $C'(t) = -kC_0 e^{-kt}$, which is proportional to the concentration itself.
- **Genetics & Allometry** — The relationship between body mass $M$ and metabolic rate $B$ follows $B = aM^{3/4}$. Differentiation gives $dB/dM = (3a/4)M^{-1/4}$, the famous **Kleiber's Law**.
- **Neuroscience** — The Hodgkin-Huxley model uses derivatives of $\exp$, $\sin$, and $\tanh$ to describe the action potential across a neuronal membrane.
- **Epidemiology** — The instantaneous infection rate is the derivative of the cumulative case count. Setting this derivative to zero gives the **inflection point** of a pandemic wave.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive-Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition is fully expanded. No step is skipped or summarized.

## 3.1 Proof of the Power Rule from First Principles

**Claim:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}\bigl(x^{n}\bigr) = n\,x^{n-1}$ for any real number $n$.

**Proof using the Binomial Theorem (for integer $n$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h)^{n} - x^{n}}{h} \quad &\text{(apply the definition)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h}\left[\binom{n}{0}x^{n} + \binom{n}{1}x^{n-1}h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n} - x^{n}\right] \quad &\text{(binomial expansion)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h}\left[n\,x^{n-1}h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}\right] \quad &\text{(cancel the } x^{n} \text{ terms)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \left[n\,x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h + \cdots + h^{n-1}\right] \quad &\text{(divide each term by } h) \\[4pt]
&= n\,x^{n-1} \quad &\text{(all terms with } h \to 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof for general real $n$ (using logarithmic differentiation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } y &= x^{n} \\
\text{Take } \ln \text{ of both sides} &\to \ln y = n \ln x \\
\text{Differentiate implicitly} &\to \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = n \cdot \frac{1}{x} \\
\text{Solve for } \frac{dy}{dx} &\to \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{n\,y}{x} = \frac{n\,x^{n}}{x} = n\,x^{n-1} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Exponential Function $e^{x}$

**Claim:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{x}) = e^{x}$.

**Proof using the limit definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dx}(e^{x}) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{x+h} - e^{x}}{h} \quad &\text{(definition)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{x}\,e^{h} - e^{x}}{h} \quad &\text{(law of exponents)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{x} \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{h} - 1}{h} \quad &\text{(factor out } e^{x}) \\[4pt]
&= e^{x} \cdot 1 \quad &\text{(since } \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{h} - 1}{h} = 1 \text{ by definition of } e) \\[4pt]
&= e^{x} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The constant $e \approx 2.71828$** is *defined* as the unique real number that makes the above limit equal to 1. This is the unique property that gives $e^{x}$ its self-derivative behaviour — making it the **only function (up to a scalar multiple) that equals its own derivative**.

## 3.3 Derivation of $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x)$ from the Limit Definition

**Claim:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(x+h) - \sin x}{h} \quad &\text{(definition)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin x \cos h + \cos x \sin h - \sin x}{h} \quad &\text{(angle-addition formula)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin x (\cos h - 1)}{h} + \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos x \sin h}{h} \quad &\text{(split the fraction)} \\[4pt]
&= \sin x \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos h - 1}{h} + \cos x \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin h}{h} \quad &\text{(factor out constants)} \\[4pt]
&= \sin x \cdot 0 + \cos x \cdot 1 \quad &\text{(standard limits)} \\[4pt]
&= \cos x \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The two **standard limits** invoked here are:

$$
\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin h}{h} = 1 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos h - 1}{h} = 0
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x)$ from the Limit Definition

**Claim:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ for $x > 0$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dx}(\ln x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\ln(x+h) - \ln x}{h} \quad &\text{(definition)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h}\ln\!\left(\frac{x+h}{x}\right) \quad &\text{(logarithm law } \ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)) \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h}\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{h}{x}\right) \quad &\text{(algebraic rearrangement)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h}\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{h}{x}\right) \cdot \frac{x}{x} \quad &\text{(multiply and divide by } x) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{x} \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\ln(1 + h/x)}{h/x} \quad &\text{(substitute } u = h/x) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{x} \cdot 1 \quad &\text{(since } \lim_{u \to 0} \frac{\ln(1+u)}{u} = 1) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{x} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Examples — Life-Science Context

### Example 1 — Bacterial Growth (Product Rule + Exponential)

The population of a bacterial culture follows $P(t) = t^{2} e^{0.3t}$ (cells, $t$ in hours). Find the growth rate $P'(t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P'(t) &= \frac{d}{dt}\bigl[t^{2}\bigr] \cdot e^{0.3t} + t^{2} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\bigl[e^{0.3t}\bigr] \quad &\text{(product rule)} \\[4pt]
&= 2t \cdot e^{0.3t} + t^{2} \cdot (0.3)\,e^{0.3t} \quad &\text{(power rule and chain rule)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{0.3t}\bigl(2t + 0.3t^{2}\bigr) \quad &\text{(factor out } e^{0.3t}) \\[4pt]
&= t\,e^{0.3t}\bigl(2 + 0.3t\bigr) \quad &\text{(final factored form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Biological interpretation:** Setting $P'(t) = 0$ gives $t = 0$ (trivial) or $2 + 0.3t = 0 \Rightarrow t = -6.67$ (non-physical, $t \geq 0$). Hence the population is **always growing** — a hallmark of unconstrained exponential growth.

### Example 2 — Drug Concentration (Quotient Rule)

A drug's plasma concentration is modelled by $C(t) = \dfrac{50t}{t^{2} + 4}$ mg/mL. Find $C'(t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C'(t) &= \frac{(50)(t^{2}+4) - (50t)(2t)}{(t^{2}+4)^{2}} \quad &\text{(quotient rule)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50t^{2} + 200 - 100t^{2}}{(t^{2}+4)^{2}} \quad &\text{(expand)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{-50t^{2} + 200}{(t^{2}+4)^{2}} \quad &\text{(collect like terms)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50(4 - t^{2})}{(t^{2}+4)^{2}} \quad &\text{(factor out 50)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Biological interpretation:** $C'(t) = 0 \Rightarrow t = 2$ hours. This is the **time of peak concentration** ($C_{\max} = 12.5$ mg/mL). For $t < 2$, $C'(t) > 0$ (absorption dominates); for $t > 2$, $C'(t) < 0$ (elimination dominates).

### Example 3 — Allometric Scaling (Chain Rule + Power Rule)

The metabolic rate of an animal is $B(M) = 70\,M^{0.75}$, where $M$ is body mass in kg. Find $dB/dM$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dB}{dM} &= 70 \cdot \frac{d}{dM}\bigl(M^{0.75}\bigr) \quad &\text{(constant multiple rule)} \\[4pt]
&= 70 \cdot 0.75 \cdot M^{0.75 - 1} \quad &\text{(power rule)} \\[4pt]
&= 52.5\,M^{-0.25} \quad &\text{(simplify)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{52.5}{M^{0.25}} = \frac{52.5}{\sqrt[4]{M}} \quad &\text{(rewrite with radical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Biological interpretation:** Metabolic rate increases with body mass but at a **decelerating rate** — a 10× mass increase only produces a $10^{0.25} \approx 1.78\times$ increase in metabolic rate. This is **Kleiber's Law** and explains why elephants do not have 100,000× the metabolic rate of mice.

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — The Differentiation Engine

The following block diagram maps the **decision flow** a student should follow when asked to differentiate a function. It is a block-level functional architecture of the differentiation process.

```mermaid
    C1 --> D1[APPLY POWER RULE N X TO N MINUS 1]
    C2 --> D2[APPLY EXP RULE E TO X TIMES F PRIME]

    C4 --> D4[APPLY CORRESPONDING TRIG DERIVATIVE]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Pattern (As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulations):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (no choice, four out of five must be answered).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer questions with **internal choice** (Module-internal choice mandatory).
> - All questions are mapped to **Course Outcomes (CO)** and **Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, CO1, L1-Remembering]`

**Define the derivative of $f(x)$ using the first-principle limit. Use this definition to find the derivative of $f(x) = 5x + 2$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The derivative of $f(x)$ at $x$ is the limit:

$$
f'(x) \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}
$$

provided the limit exists.

**Computation for $f(x) = 5x + 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{[5(x+h) + 2] - [5x + 2]}{h} \quad &\text{(substitute } f(x+h) \text{ and } f(x)) \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{5x + 5h + 2 - 5x - 2}{h} \quad &\text{(distribute and expand)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{5h}{h} \quad &\text{(cancel common terms)} \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{h \to 0} 5 \quad &\text{(simplify the fraction)} \\[4pt]
&= 5 \quad &\text{(limit of a constant is itself)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This is a **3-mark standard question**. Marks are awarded: **1 mark** for the correct definition, **1.5 marks** for the algebraic expansion, and **0.5 mark** for the final numerical answer.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper, CO2, L2-Understanding]`

**Differentiate $y = e^{3x} \sin(2x)$ with respect to $x$.**

**Model Answer:**

Using the **product rule** with $u = e^{3x}$ and $v = \sin(2x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{du}{dx} \cdot v + u \cdot \frac{dv}{dx} \quad &\text{(product rule)} \\[4pt]
&= (3e^{3x})(\sin 2x) + (e^{3x})(2\cos 2x) \quad &\text{(chain rule for each factor)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{3x}\bigl[3\sin(2x) + 2\cos(2x)\bigr] \quad &\text{(factor out } e^{3x})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-Internal Choice Rule:** The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (not both). Both questions must be from **Module 2** and carry identical marks distribution (typically **7 marks per sub-part**).

---

### Question A `[14 Marks, CO2/CO3, L3-Applying & L4-Analyzing]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ for the following functions using appropriate differentiation rules:
  (i) $y = \dfrac{\ln x}{x^{2} + 1}$
  (ii) $y = \sqrt{1 + \sin^{2}(x)}$

**(b) [7 Marks]** The population of a bacteria colony in a petri dish is modelled by the logistic function $P(t) = \dfrac{1000}{1 + 9e^{-0.5t}}$, where $t$ is in hours. Find:
  (i) The growth rate function $P'(t)$.
  (ii) The maximum sustainable population (carrying capacity).
  (iii) The inflection point of the growth curve.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) $y = \dfrac{\ln x}{x^{2} + 1}$ — Quotient Rule:**

Let $f(x) = \ln x$ and $g(x) = x^{2} + 1$. Then $f'(x) = 1/x$ and $g'(x) = 2x$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{f'(x) g(x) - f(x) g'(x)}{[g(x)]^{2}} \quad &\text{(quotient rule)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(1/x)(x^{2}+1) - (\ln x)(2x)}{(x^{2}+1)^{2}} \quad &\text{(substitute)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\frac{x^{2}+1}{x} - 2x \ln x}{(x^{2}+1)^{2}} \quad &\text{(simplify numerator)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{x^{2} + 1 - 2x^{2} \ln x}{x\,(x^{2}+1)^{2}} \quad &\text{(combine over common denominator } x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) $y = \sqrt{1 + \sin^{2}(x)}$ — Chain Rule:**

Let $u = 1 + \sin^{2}x$. Then $y = u^{1/2}$ and $dy/du = (1/2)u^{-1/2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{du}{dx} &= 2 \sin x \cdot \cos x = \sin(2x) \quad &\text{(chain rule + double-angle identity)} \\[4pt]
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1 + \sin^{2}x}} \cdot \sin(2x) \quad &\text{(chain rule)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\sin(2x)}{2\sqrt{1 + \sin^{2}x}} \quad &\text{(final simplified form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Differentiating the logistic function:**

We rewrite $P(t) = 1000 \bigl[1 + 9e^{-0.5t}\bigr]^{-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P'(t) &= -1000 \bigl[1 + 9e^{-0.5t}\bigr]^{-2} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\bigl[1 + 9e^{-0.5t}\bigr] \quad &\text{(chain rule)} \\[4pt]
&= -1000 \bigl[1 + 9e^{-0.5t}\bigr]^{-2} \cdot \bigl(-4.5 e^{-0.5t}\bigr) \quad &\text{(differentiate inner)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4500\,e^{-0.5t}}{\bigl[1 + 9e^{-0.5t}\bigr]^{2}} \quad &\text{(simplify signs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Carrying capacity:**

Taking $\lim_{t \to \infty}$:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} P(t) = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{1000}{1 + 9e^{-0.5t}} = \frac{1000}{1 + 0} = 1000
$$

The maximum sustainable population is $K = 1000$ bacteria.

**(iii) Inflection point:**

The inflection point occurs where $P'(t)$ attains its **maximum**, which for a logistic curve happens at $P = K/2 = 500$. Setting $P(t) = 500$:

$$
500 = \frac{1000}{1 + 9e^{-0.5t}} \Rightarrow 1 + 9e^{-0.5t} = 2 \Rightarrow 9e^{-0.5t} = 1 \Rightarrow e^{-0.5t} = \frac{1}{9}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln 9}{0.5} = 2\ln 9 \approx 4.39 \text{ hours}
$$

The inflection point is at $t \approx 4.39$ hours, $P = 500$ bacteria.

---

### Question B `[14 Marks, CO2/CO3, L3-Applying & L4-Analyzing]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate the following with respect to $x$:
  (i) $y = x^{x}$ (use logarithmic differentiation).
  (ii) $y = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1 + x}{1 - x}\right)$ for $x \neq 1$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A patient is administered a drug intravenously. The concentration in the bloodstream is $C(t) = 20\,e^{-0.15t}\sin(0.8t)$ mg/L, where $t$ is in hours. Find:
  (i) $C'(t)$ (the rate of change of concentration).
  (ii) The time at which the concentration first reaches its **maximum**.
  (iii) The value of $C_{\max}$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) $y = x^{x}$ — Logarithmic Differentiation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln y &= x \ln x \quad &\text{(take log of both sides)} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} &= 1 \cdot \ln x + x \cdot \frac{1}{x} \quad &\text{(product rule on RHS)} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} &= \ln x + 1 \\[4pt]
\frac{dy}{dx} &= y(\ln x + 1) = x^{x}(1 + \ln x) \quad &\text{(substitute back } y = x^{x})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) $y = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$:**

Recall the identity: $\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1+x}{1-x}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1) + \tan^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} + \tan^{-1}(x)$ for $x < 1$.

Differentiating directly using the chain rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)^{2}} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right) \quad &\text{(chain rule)} \\[4pt]
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right) &= \frac{(1)(1-x) - (1+x)(-1)}{(1-x)^{2}} = \frac{2}{(1-x)^{2}} \quad &\text{(quotient rule)} \\[4pt]
1 + \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)^{2} &= \frac{(1-x)^{2} + (1+x)^{2}}{(1-x)^{2}} = \frac{2(1+x^{2})}{(1-x)^{2}} \\[4pt]
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{(1-x)^{2}}{2(1+x^{2})} \cdot \frac{2}{(1-x)^{2}} = \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \quad &\text{(everything cancels)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Differentiating $C(t) = 20 e^{-0.15t} \sin(0.8t)$ — Product Rule + Chain Rule:**

Let $u = 20e^{-0.15t}$ and $v = \sin(0.8t)$. Then $u' = -3e^{-0.15t}$ and $v' = 0.8\cos(0.8t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C'(t) &= u'v + uv' \\[4pt]
&= (-3e^{-0.15t})\sin(0.8t) + (20e^{-0.15t})(0.8\cos(0.8t)) \\[4pt]
&= e^{-0.15t}\bigl[-3\sin(0.8t) + 16\cos(0.8t)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= e^{-0.15t}\bigl[16\cos(0.8t) - 3\sin(0.8t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Setting $C'(t) = 0$ for the first maximum:**

$$
16\cos(0.8t) - 3\sin(0.8t) = 0 \Rightarrow \tan(0.8t) = \frac{16}{3} \Rightarrow 0.8t = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{16}{3}\right) \approx 1.3844
$$

$$
t \approx \frac{1.3844}{0.8} \approx 1.73 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Computing $C_{\max}$:**

$$
C(1.73) = 20 \cdot e^{-0.15 \times 1.73} \cdot \sin(0.8 \times 1.73) = 20 \cdot e^{-0.2595} \cdot \sin(1.384)
$$

$$
C_{\max} \approx 20 \cdot 0.7715 \cdot 0.9830 \approx 15.17 \text{ mg/L}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Module 2:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Quotient Rule** — Students frequently write the numerator as $f' g + f g'$ (product rule form) instead of the correct $\mathbf{f' g - f g'}$. This is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Chain Rule in Transcendental Functions** — When differentiating $e^{3x}$, students write $e^{3x}$ instead of $3e^{3x}$. Always multiply by the derivative of the exponent.
> 3. **Domain of $\ln x$ ignored** — When differentiating $\ln f(x)$, students forget the **inner derivative** and write $1/f(x)$ instead of $f'(x)/f(x)$.
> 4. **Misapplication of Power Rule** — For $x^{x}$, the power rule **does not apply** (variable base, variable exponent). Use logarithmic differentiation.
> 5. **Forgetting the absolute value in inverse trig derivatives** — For $\sec^{-1}(x)$ and $\csc^{-1}(x)$, the derivative must contain $\vert x \vert$ in the denominator. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.
> 6. **In Life-Science Word Problems** — Students compute the derivative but forget to **interpret the result** (e.g., the meaning of $C'(t) = 0$ at $t = 2$ being the time of peak concentration). Always close with a biological interpretation.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision card.**

- The **derivative** is the **instantaneous rate of change** of $f(x)$ with respect to $x$.
- The **Power Rule**: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{n}) = n x^{n-1}$ — works for **all** real $n$.
- The **Exponential Rule**: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{x}) = e^{x}$ — the unique function that is its own derivative.
- The **Logarithm Rule**: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ for $x > 0$.
- All **trigonometric derivatives** follow the cyclic pattern: $\sin \to \cos \to -\sin \to -\cos \to \sin$ with a period of 4 and a sign of $(+,-,-,+)$ for the four co-function pairs.
- The **sum rule** is additive; the **product rule** is *not* simply the product of derivatives.
- The **quotient rule** has a **minus** sign in the numerator — never forget it.
- The **chain rule** multiplies rates: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy}{du} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx}$ — used in virtually every applied problem.
- For **inverse trig** derivatives, remember the domain restrictions: $\vert x \vert < 1$ for $\sin^{-1}$ and $\cos^{-1}$; $\vert x \vert > 1$ for $\sec^{-1}$ and $\csc^{-1}$.
- For **hyperbolic** derivatives, $\sinh$ and $\cosh$ are like $\sin$ and $\cos$ but **without the alternating signs**.
- For **logarithmic differentiation** of $y = f(x)^{g(x)}$, take $\ln$, use the product rule, then solve for $y'$.
- In **life-science applications**, always interpret the derivative: $P'(t) = 0 \to$ steady state; $P'(t) > 0 \to$ growth; $P'(t) < 0 \to$ decay.
- The **carrying capacity** of a logistic model $P(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + Ae^{-rt}}$ is the horizontal asymptote $K$.
- The **inflection point** of a logistic curve occurs at $P = K/2$, where growth rate is maximum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE -1
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Differentiation of a Product

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Rule (Leibniz's Product Rule):**
> If $u = u(x)$ and $v = v(x)$ are two differentiable functions of a single independent variable $x$, then the derivative of their product $y = u \cdot v$ with respect to $x$ is given by:
> $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\bigl(u \cdot v\bigr) = u \frac{dv}{dx} + v \frac{du}{dx}$$
> Equivalently, using the prime (Newton's) notation: $y' = u'v + uv'$.

This is one of the foundational **rules of differentiation** introduced after the study of derivatives of standard functions, trigonometric functions, and the **sum/difference rule**. It is essential for differentiating compound expressions arising in biological modelling, pharmacology, and ecological systems.

### 🌱 Conceptual Analogy (Life-Science Intuition)

Imagine a **rectangular patch of forest** whose length $L(t)$ and width $W(t)$ are both growing with time $t$.

- The **area** of the patch is $A(t) = L(t) \times W(t)$.
- You want to know **how fast the area is growing**, i.e., $\dfrac{dA}{dt}$.
- The area grows because:
  1. The **length is increasing** while width stays (instantaneously) fixed → contributes $L'(t) \cdot W(t)$.
  2. The **width is increasing** while length stays (instantaneously) fixed → contributes $L(t) \cdot W'(t)$.

So the total rate of change is the **sum of these two independent contributions** — neither one alone captures the full growth. That is precisely the **Product Rule**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for life-science students:** Whenever a quantity is the *product* of two changing variables (e.g., *concentration × volume*, *population density × area*, *metabolic rate × body mass*), the rate of change is **not simply the product of the rates** — it is the **sum of two cross-terms**.

### 📐 Standard Notations Used

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $u, v$ | Two differentiable functions of $x$ |
| $u', v'$ | First derivatives of $u$ and $v$ with respect to $x$ |
| $\dfrac{du}{dx}, \dfrac{dv}{dx}$ | Differential-notation derivatives |
| $y$ | The product function: $y = u \cdot v$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 Step-by-Step Logic of the Product Rule

The Product Rule is built on a clear, repeatable reasoning pattern. Memorise the **structure**, not just the formula:

1. **Identify** the two functions $u(x)$ and $v(x)$ being multiplied.
2. **Differentiate** each function independently to get $u'(x)$ and $v'(x)$.
3. **Cross-multiply** the derivative of one with the *original* form of the other.
4. **Add** the two cross-products.
5. **Simplify** algebraically where possible.

> [!TIP]
> A popular student mnemonic is **"First × Derivative of Second + Second × Derivative of First"**, often remembered as *"leave one, differentiate one, then add the switch"*.

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind the Rule

The derivative measures **instantaneous sensitivity**. When two quantities vary simultaneously, the change in their product is the **combined effect** of:

- Change in $u$ multiplied by the **current** value of $v$, **plus**
- Change in $v$ multiplied by the **current** value of $u$.

The infinitesimal higher-order term $du \cdot dv$ is **negligible** in the limit (it vanishes as $dx \to 0$), which is why we see only the two linear cross-terms and **not** a $\frac{du}{dx} \cdot \frac{dv}{dx}$ term.

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in Life Sciences |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(uv) = u\dfrac{dv}{dx} + v\dfrac{du}{dx}$ | **Product Rule** (basic) | Differentiating product of two functions |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\bigl[f(x)\,g(x)\,h(x)\bigr] = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'$ | **Extension to three factors** | Volume × density × specific heat |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\bigl[c \cdot u(x)\bigr] = c \cdot u'(x)$ | Constant-Multiple Rule (corollary) | Drug dose $c$ × rate function |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\bigl[u(x)\bigr]^{n} = n\,u^{n-1}\,u'$ | Generalized Power Rule with Product | Allometric scaling $y = aW^{b}$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\bigl(u \cdot v \cdot w\bigr) = (uvw)' = u'vw + uv'w + uvw'$ | Symmetric 3-factor product | Cell mass = density × volume |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common misconception:** Students often wrongly write $\dfrac{d}{dx}(uv) = \dfrac{du}{dx} \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dx}$. This is **FALSE**. The correct expression is the **sum** of two **cross-terms**, not a single product of derivatives. The product of derivatives is only a *second-order infinitesimal* and is discarded in the limit.

### 🌍 Real-World Utility in Life-Science Engineering

- **Pharmacokinetics:** The total amount of drug in plasma is $A(t) = C(t) \cdot V_d$, where $C(t)$ is concentration and $V_d$ is volume of distribution. Differentiating $A$ uses the Product Rule.
- **Ecology:** Total population $P = D \times A$, where $D$ is density and $A$ is habitat area. Rate of change in $P$ requires product differentiation.
- **Bioenergetics:** Metabolic power $P = \dot{V}O_2 \times \text{kcal per L of } O_2$ — both factors may change.
- **Microbial Growth:** Biomass $B(t) = N(t) \cdot m(t)$ (cell number × mean cell mass) — product differentiation gives total biomass production rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Exhaustive Proof of the Product Rule (First Principles)

We derive $\dfrac{d}{dx}(uv) = u\dfrac{dv}{dx} + v\dfrac{du}{dx}$ directly from the **limit definition** of the derivative. Every step is shown explicitly.

### ✅ Setup

Let $y = u(x) \cdot v(x)$, where $u$ and $v$ are differentiable at $x$. Give $x$ a small increment $\delta x$, causing $u$ to change by $\delta u$ and $v$ to change by $\delta v$. The new product is:

$$y + \delta y = (u + \delta u)(v + \delta v)$$

### ✅ Step 1 — Expand the Right-Hand Side

$$y + \delta y = u v + u \cdot \delta v + v \cdot \delta u + \delta u \cdot \delta v$$

### ✅ Step 2 — Subtract $y = uv$ from Both Sides

$$\delta y = u \cdot \delta v + v \cdot \delta u + \delta u \cdot \delta v$$

### ✅ Step 3 — Divide Throughout by $\delta x$

$$\frac{\delta y}{\delta x} = u \cdot \frac{\delta v}{\delta x} + v \cdot \frac{\delta u}{\delta x} + \frac{\delta u}{\delta x} \cdot \delta v$$

### ✅ Step 4 — Take the Limit as $\delta x \to 0$

$$\lim_{\delta x \to 0}\frac{\delta y}{\delta x} = u \cdot \lim_{\delta x \to 0}\frac{\delta v}{\delta x} + v \cdot \lim_{\delta x \to 0}\frac{\delta u}{\delta x} + \lim_{\delta x \to 0}\left(\frac{\delta u}{\delta x} \cdot \delta v\right)$$

### ✅ Step 5 — Apply Known Limits

Since $u$ and $v$ are differentiable, they are also continuous. Therefore as $\delta x \to 0$, $\delta v \to 0$. The third limit becomes:

$$\lim_{\delta x \to 0}\left(\frac{\delta u}{\delta x}\right) \cdot \lim_{\delta x \to 0}(\delta v) = \frac{du}{dx} \cdot 0 = 0$$

Hence:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = u \cdot \frac{dv}{dx} + v \cdot \frac{du}{dx} + 0$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d}{dx}(u \cdot v) = u \frac{dv}{dx} + v \frac{du}{dx}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $\delta u \cdot \delta v$ term vanishes:** It is a product of two *first-order infinitesimals* ($\delta u \propto \delta x$, $\delta v \propto \delta x$), so it is of order $(\delta x)^2$ — it shrinks to zero much faster than $\delta x$ itself and is therefore irrelevant in the limit.

---

## 📝 Worked Examples (Model Solutions)

### 🔬 Example 1 — Basic Two-Factor Product

**Problem:** Differentiate $y = (3x^2 + 1)(2x - 5)$ with respect to $x$.

**Solution:**

Identify $u = 3x^2 + 1$ and $v = 2x - 5$.

Compute derivatives:

$$u' = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 + 1) = 6x$$

$$v' = \frac{d}{dx}(2x - 5) = 2$$

Apply the Product Rule:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = u v' + v u'$$

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = (3x^2 + 1)(2) + (2x - 5)(6x)$$

Expand each term:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 6x^2 + 2 + 12x^2 - 30x$$

Combine like terms:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 18x^2 - 30x + 2$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 18x^2 - 30x + 2$.

---

### 🌿 Example 2 — Trigonometric × Polynomial Product

**Problem:** Differentiate $y = x^2 \sin x$ with respect to $x$.

**Solution:**

Identify $u = x^2$ and $v = \sin x$.

Compute derivatives:

$$u' = 2x, \qquad v' = \cos x$$

Apply the Product Rule:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = u v' + v u' = (x^2)(\cos x) + (\sin x)(2x)$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2 \cos x + 2x \sin x$.

---

### 🧬 Example 3 — Exponential × Polynomial (Life-Science Application)

**Problem:** A bacterial population follows $P(t) = t^2 e^{0.3t}$ (cells, $t$ in hours). Find the growth rate $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$.

**Solution:**

Let $u = t^2$ and $v = e^{0.3t}$.

Derivatives:

$$u' = 2t$$

$$v' = 0.3\, e^{0.3t}$$

Product Rule:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = (t^2)(0.3 e^{0.3t}) + (e^{0.3t})(2t)$$

Factor $e^{0.3t}$:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = e^{0.3t}\bigl(0.3 t^2 + 2t\bigr)$$

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = t\, e^{0.3t}\bigl(0.3 t + 2\bigr)$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = t\, e^{0.3t}(0.3t + 2)$ cells/hour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Life-science interpretation:** At $t=0$, the growth rate is $0$ (population starts from a single founder, but the polynomial factor vanishes). For $t>0$, the exponential term dominates and growth accelerates.

---

### 🧫 Example 4 — Three-Factor Product (Extension)

**Problem:** Differentiate $y = x \cdot \sin x \cdot e^x$ with respect to $x$.

**Solution:**

For three factors $f \cdot g \cdot h$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'$$

Let $f = x$, $g = \sin x$, $h = e^x$. Derivatives: $f' = 1$, $g' = \cos x$, $h' = e^x$.

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = (1)(\sin x)(e^x) + (x)(\cos x)(e^x) + (x)(\sin x)(e^x)$$

Factor $e^x$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = e^x \bigl(\sin x + x\cos x + x\sin x\bigr)$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = e^x\bigl(\sin x + x\cos x + x\sin x\bigr)$.

---

### 🧠 Example 5 — Logarithmic × Exponential (Challenging)

**Problem:** Differentiate $y = \ln x \cdot e^x$ with respect to $x$ (for $x > 0$).

**Solution:**

Let $u = \ln x$ and $v = e^x$. Then $u' = \dfrac{1}{x}$ and $v' = e^x$.

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = (\ln x)(e^x) + (e^x)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$$

Factor $e^x$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = e^x\left(\ln x + \frac{1}{x}\right)$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = e^x\!\left(\ln x + \dfrac{1}{x}\right)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ Block-Level Functional Flow of the Product Rule

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **decision and execution flow** a student must follow when applying the Product Rule, with explicit cross-terms and final simplification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Given: y = u of x times v of x]) --> id1[Step 1: Identify u of x and v of x]
    id1 --> id2[Step 2: Compute u prime of x]
    id1 --> id3[Step 3: Compute v prime of x]
    id2 --> crossA[Term A: u of x times v prime of x]
    id3 --> crossB[Term B: v of x times u prime of x]
    id3 --> crossA
    id2 --> crossB
    crossA --> sum[Step 4: Add Term A and Term B]
    crossB --> sum
    sum --> simp[Step 5: Factor or Simplify]
    simp --> final([Final: dy by dx = u dv by dx + v du by dx])
    style start fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style final fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style id1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style id2 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style id3 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style crossA fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style crossB fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style sum fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    style simp fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
```

## 🔁 Three-Factor Product Rule Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    func[Function: F of x = f of x times g of x times h of x] --> d1[Differentiate f only]
    func --> d2[Differentiate g only]
    func --> d3[Differentiate h only]
    d1 --> t1[Term 1: f prime of x times g of x times h of x]
    d2 --> t2[Term 2: f of x times g prime of x times h of x]
    d3 --> t3[Term 3: f of x times g of x times h prime of x]
    t1 --> sum[Add all three terms]
    t2 --> sum
    t3 --> sum
    sum --> res[Result: dF by dx = f prime g h + f g prime h + f g h prime]
```
