# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the total differential of a function $z = f(x, y)$. State the conditions under which it exists.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The total differential of $z = f(x, y)$ is defined as:

$$dz = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\,dy$$

**Conditions for existence:**
1. The function $f(x, y)$ must be **continuously differentiable** (i.e., $f \in C^1$) on the domain.
2. The first-order partial derivatives $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ and $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ must **exist** in a neighbourhood of the point and be **continuous** at the point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for conditions. (Standard KTU valuation key.)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the exactness condition for the differential form $M\,dx + N\,dy = 0$. Mention its significance in life science applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Exactness Condition:** The differential form $M(x, y)\,dx + N(x, y)\,dy = 0$ is said to be **exact** if there exists a function $F(x, y)$ such that $dF = M\,dx + N\,dy$. The necessary and sufficient condition is:

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}$$

**Significance in Life Sciences:**
- Identifies **state functions** in bio-thermodynamics (internal energy $U$, enthalpy $H$, Gibbs free energy $G$, entropy $S$).
- Forms the basis of **Maxwell relations** linking partial derivatives of thermodynamic potentials.
- Allows integration of biological conservation laws to obtain potential energy landscapes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 1 mark statement, 1 mark formula, 1 mark significance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module-level questions carry **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define the total differential of a function $z = f(x, y)$ and derive the formula $dz = f_x\,dx + f_y\,dy$ from the increment form. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A population grows according to $N = f(P, T)$, where $P$ is the food supply (kg) and $T$ is the temperature (°C). At a particular instant, $P = 100\,\text{kg}$, $T = 25\,{}^\circ\text{C}$. The partial derivatives are estimated as $\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial P} = 2.5$ and $\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial T} = -1.2$. Compute the total differential $dN$ when $dP = 4\,\text{kg}$ and $dT = 0.5\,{}^\circ\text{C}$. Interpret the sign of the result. **\[7 marks\]**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The total differential of $z = f(x, y)$ is the linear combination of differentials $dx$ and $dy$ weighted by the corresponding first-order partial derivatives. It represents the best linear approximation to the change in $f$ for infinitesimal independent increments $dx$ and $dy$.

**Derivation (5 marks):** See Section 3.1 above for the full five-step derivation from the increment form. Key steps:

1. Write $\Delta z = f(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y) - f(x, y)$.
2. Add and subtract the intermediate term $f(x, y + \Delta y)$.
3. Apply the single-variable Mean Value Theorem to each bracket.
4. Take the limit $\Delta x, \Delta y \to 0$, using continuity of partial derivatives.
5. Identify $dz$, $dx$, $dy$ as the limits of $\Delta z$, $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$.

Final boxed result: $dz = f_x\,dx + f_y\,dy$.

---

**Part (b) — Application (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the total differential formula (1 mark):**

$$dN = \frac{\partial N}{\partial P}\,dP + \frac{\partial N}{\partial T}\,dT$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values (3 marks):**

$$dN = (2.5)(4) + (-1.2)(0.5)$$

$$dN = 10.0 - 0.6 = 9.4$$

**Step 3 — Unit analysis (1 mark):**
$\left[\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial P}\right] = \text{population/kg}$ (so $f_P \cdot dP$ has units of population). ✓ Similarly $f_T \cdot dT$ has units of population. The total $dN$ is in **population units** (e.g., number of individuals).

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
The positive value $dN = +9.4$ indicates that the population **increases by approximately 9.4 individuals** when food supply rises by $4\,\text{kg}$ and temperature rises by $0.5\,{}^\circ\text{C}$ simultaneously. The dominant positive contribution comes from increased food (10.0 individuals), partially offset by the negative effect of temperature rise (-0.6 individuals). This reflects the ecological trade-off between resource availability and thermal stress on the population.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **exact** and **inexact** differentials with suitable examples from thermodynamics. State the exactness test and verify it for the form $(2xy + 3)\,dx + (x^2 + 4y)\,dy$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** The Gibbs free energy of a biological system is $G = G(T, P) = 5T^2 - 3TP + 2P^2$ (in suitable units), where $T$ is temperature and $P$ is pressure. Compute the total differential $dG$ and show that $G$ is a state function. **\[7 marks\]**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Exact vs Inexact Differentials (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
- An **exact differential** $dz$ of a function $z = f(x, y)$ satisfies the exactness condition $\partial M/\partial y = \partial N/\partial x$, where $M = f_x$ and $N = f_y$. Examples: $dU$ (internal energy), $dH$ (enthalpy), $dG$ (Gibbs free energy), $dS$ (entropy).
- An **inexact differential** is denoted $\delta$ (not $d$) because it cannot be expressed as the differential of a single-valued state function. Examples: $\delta Q$ (heat), $\delta W$ (work).

**Exactness Test (1 mark):** $\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}$

**Verification (3 marks):**
- $M = 2xy + 3 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y} = 2x$
- $N = x^2 + 4y \Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x} = 2x$
- Since $2x = 2x$, the form is **exact** ✓

---

**Part (b) — Total Differential of Gibbs Free Energy (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute partial derivatives (3 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial G}{\partial T} = 10T - 3P, \qquad \frac{\partial G}{\partial P} = -3T + 4P$$

**Step 2 — Apply the total differential formula (2 marks):**

$$dG = \frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\,dT + \frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\,dP$$

$$dG = (10T - 3P)\,dT + (-3T + 4P)\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Verify that $G$ is a state function via exactness (2 marks):**
- $M = 10T - 3P \Rightarrow \partial M/\partial P = -3$
- $N = -3T + 4P \Rightarrow \partial N/\partial T = -3$
- Cross-partials match, so $dG$ is an **exact differential**. This confirms that $G$ is a **state function** of $(T, P)$ — its change between two thermodynamic states is path-independent, which is the cornerstone of bio-thermodynamic analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Chain Rule:** When applying $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = f_x \dot{x} + f_y \dot{y}$, students frequently forget to multiply by $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$. **Always carry the time-derivatives explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Exact vs Inexact Notation:** The examiner strictly penalises writing $dQ$ instead of $\delta Q$ for an inexact differential. Use $d$ **only** for state functions, and $\delta$ for path-dependent quantities.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Continuity Assumption:** A common 1-mark loss in definitions is omitting the phrase "assuming $f_x$ and $f_y$ are continuous." This is a KTU board examiner's standard deduction.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency in Application Problems:** When computing $dN$ or $dG$, always check that the units of each term are consistent. Examiners often assign **partial credit** only if units are explicitly verified.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in the Second Differential:** The mixed term in $d^2 z$ is $\mathbf{2}\,f_{xy}\,dx\,dy$, **not** $f_{xy}\,dx\,dy$. This is a high-frequency error and costs full marks on that sub-part.
>
> 6. **In the Exactness Test:** Always show **both** partial derivatives explicitly. Writing only one of $\partial M/\partial y$ or $\partial N/\partial x$ without the other will be marked incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Total Differential (Module 2)**

- **Core Formula:** $dz = f_x\,dx + f_y\,dy$ — the linear approximation to $\Delta z$.
- **Extension to $n$ variables:** $du = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{x_i}\,dx_i = \nabla f \cdot d\mathbf{x}$.
- **Total Time-Derivative:** $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = f_x \dot{x} + f_y \dot{y}$ (chain rule in differential form).
- **Exactness Test:** $\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}$ — necessary and sufficient for $M\,dx + N\,dy$ to be exact.
- **Exact vs Inexact:** Use $d$ for state functions ($U, H, G, S$); use $\delta$ for path-dependent quantities ($Q, W$).
- **Differential Algebra Rules:** $d(cu) = c\,du$; $d(u + v) = du + dv$; $d(uv) = u\,dv + v\,du$; $d(u^n) = n\,u^{n-1}\,du$.
- **Second Total Differential:** $d^2 z = f_{xx}\,dx^2 + 2f_{xy}\,dx\,dy + f_{yy}\,dy^2$ — the mixed term has a factor of **2**.
- **Error Propagation:** $\Delta z \approx f_x \Delta x + f_y \Delta y$ — used in clinical and laboratory measurement uncertainty analysis.
- **Existence Conditions:** $f \in C^1$ (continuously differentiable) on the domain.
- **Geometric Meaning:** $dz$ is the change along the **tangent plane** to the surface $z = f(x, y)$; $\Delta z - dz$ is the **second-order residual**.
- **Life-Science Hot Topics:** Pharmacokinetics (drug concentration), population genetics (allele frequency), bio-thermodynamics (state functions), epidemiology (sensitivity of infection rate), enzyme kinetics (Michaelis–Menten sensitivity), ecological modelling (time-varying parameters).
- **Common Exam Traps:** Sign errors, missing continuity assumption, unit mismatches, factor-of-2 omission in $d^2 z$, misusing $d$ vs $\delta$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR LIFE SCIENCE -1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rates of Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **GDMAT101 – Mathematics for Life Science – 1**, the topic **"Rates of Change"** is formally defined as the mathematical study of how one biological or physical quantity varies with respect to another, typically measured per unit of time. Formally, the **rate of change of a function $f(t)$** with respect to the independent variable $t$ is given by the **first derivative**:

$$
\frac{df}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{f(t + \Delta t) - f(t)}{\Delta t}
$$

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned), this topic is classified under **Course Outcome CO2** of Module 2, requiring students to *“apply the concept of derivatives to model and analyze rate-based phenomena in life sciences.”*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GDMAT101, Module 2):**
> Rates of change forms the analytical bridge between **static mathematical functions** and **dynamic biological processes** such as population dynamics, enzyme kinetics, drug concentration decay, and cellular growth.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **bacterial colony grow** in a Petri dish under a microscope. At 9:00 AM, the colony has $1{,}000$ bacteria. At 9:01 AM, it has $1{,}020$ bacteria. The **average rate** tells you it grew by $20$ bacteria per minute. But the **instantaneous rate of change** is the *exact* speed at which the colony is growing *right now* — like reading the speedometer of a car at a single instant, not averaging over an hour.

In life sciences, this matters because:
- **Too slow a growth** → the species may go extinct.
- **Too fast a decline** (e.g., of a hormone concentration) → a patient may be in danger.
- The **sign** of the rate (+ or −) tells us whether the quantity is **increasing or decreasing**.

| Real-Life Analogy | Mathematical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Speed of a car at an instant | Instantaneous rate of change |
| Average speed over a journey | Average rate of change |
| Speedometer reading | Value of $f'(t)$ |
| Speedometer going backward | $f'(t) < 0$ (decreasing function) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> A *rate of change* answers the question: *“How fast is something changing, and in which direction?”* The derivative $f'(t)$ is the precise mathematical tool to answer this for any smooth function.

---

## 1.3 Standard Terminology in KTU 2024 Context

| Term | Meaning | Biological Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Average Rate of Change** | $\dfrac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$ | Mean growth of population from 2020 to 2024 |
| **Instantaneous Rate of Change** | $f'(a) = \lim\limits_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}$ | Growth rate of a tumour at the moment of diagnosis |
| **Increasing Function** | $f'(x) > 0$ | Bacterial population expanding |
| **Decreasing Function** | $f'(x) < 0$ | Drug concentration in bloodstream after peak |
| **Marginal Quantity** | Approximate change for a unit increase | Marginal cost of producing one more vaccine dose |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU ESE papers, examiners frequently ask: *“Interpret the sign of the derivative in a biological context.”* Always tie your answer back to a **biological meaning** (e.g., *“positive derivative implies the population is still growing”*).

---

## 1.4 Why "Rates of Change" Is Central to Life Science Mathematics

Life sciences are inherently **dynamic** — cells divide, populations evolve, enzymes react, neurons fire. The static equations of algebra cannot capture motion; we need the **calculus of change**. The four pillars of life-science mathematics that depend on rates of change are:

1. **Population Dynamics** — Malthusian and logistic growth models.
2. **Pharmacokinetics** — Drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME).
3. **Epidemiology** — Rate of spread of infectious diseases ($R_0$).
4. **Biomechanics & Physiology** — Rate of oxygen uptake, blood flow velocity, nerve impulse transmission.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The mathematical machinery of "rates of change" rests on **four conceptual pillars**, each of which the KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to master:

### Pillar 1: The Difference Quotient (Foundation Stone)

The difference quotient is the **algebraic starting point** of every derivative problem.

$$
\text{Difference Quotient} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{f(x + h) - f(x)}{h}
$$

> **Why it matters:** The difference quotient is the *average* rate of change. When we take the **limit** as $h \to 0$, we convert the average into an **instantaneous** rate.

### Pillar 2: The Limiting Process (Bridge to the Derivative)

Taking the limit makes the average rate "shrink" to a single point. This is the most subtle step in calculus:

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}
$$

- If the limit **exists** (finite), then $f$ is **differentiable** at $x$.
- If the limit is $\pm \infty$, we have a **vertical tangent** (e.g., cusp in a population crash).
- If the limit **does not exist**, the function has a **corner or discontinuity** (e.g., sudden species extinction event).

### Pillar 3: Standard Differentiation Rules (Computational Toolkit)

Rather than always using the limiting definition, KTU expects fluency with these rules:

| Rule | Formula | Biological Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Rule** | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = n \cdot x^{n-1}$ | Allometric scaling laws ($y = a \cdot x^{3/4}$) |
| **Constant Multiple** | $\dfrac{d}{dx}[k \cdot f(x)] = k \cdot f'(x)$ | Scaling a population model |
| **Sum Rule** | $\dfrac{d}{dx}[f(x) \pm g(x)] = f'(x) \pm g'(x)$ | Combined growth + decay |
| **Product Rule** | $\dfrac{d}{dx}[u \cdot v] = u'v + uv'$ | Two interacting species |
| **Quotient Rule** | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\dfrac{u}{v}\right] = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$ | Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics |
| **Chain Rule** | $\dfrac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$ | Nested feedback loops |

### Pillar 4: The Sign and Magnitude Interpretation

In life science, **the derivative is not just a number — it is a story**:

- **Sign of $f'(x)$** → direction of change (growing / shrinking / stable).
- **Magnitude of $f'(x)$** → how rapid the change is.
- **$f'(x) = 0$** → **critical point** (e.g., carrying capacity of an ecosystem, equilibrium concentration).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize it before every KTU ESE attempt.

| # | Formula | Condition / Domain | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f'(x) = \lim\limits_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ | $h \neq 0$ | First-principles definition |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(c) = 0$ | $c \in \mathbb{R}$ | Constant (e.g., baseline temperature) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}$ | $n \in \mathbb{R}$ | Power-law biology |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^x) = e^x$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ | Exponential growth/decay |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x) = a^x \ln a$ | $a > 0$ | Bacterial replication |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ | $x > 0$ | Logarithmic decay |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$ | $x$ in radians | Oscillatory neural signals |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\cos x) = -\sin x$ | $x$ in radians | Wave-based biology |
| 9 | $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$ | Both differentiable | Predator–prey interactions |
| 10 | $\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)' = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$ | $v \neq 0$ | Enzyme-substrate ratio |
| 11 | $[f(g(x))]' = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$ | Composition differentiable | Nested biological models |
| 12 | $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = k \cdot y$ | Solution: $y = y_0 e^{kt}$ | Malthusian growth |

> **Critical Notation Rule (KTU 2024):** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ in math is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables. This is also how we write the **absolute value** $\vert x \vert$ of a quantity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Life-Science Utility

The concept of rates of change underpins:

- **Biomedical Engineering:** Real-time monitoring of heart rate variability ($d\text{HR}/dt$).
- **Pharmacology:** Calculating infusion rates to maintain steady drug plasma levels.
- **Ecology:** Predicting species extinction timelines via $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{K}\right)$ (logistic).
- **Neuroscience:** Modeling action potentials as $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ — the **rate of decay** $\tau$ is critical.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: First-Principles Derivative of $f(x) = x^2$

This is a **mandatory KTU 2024 question pattern** — examiners expect students to derive basic derivatives from the limit definition.

**Problem:** Find $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2)$ using the first-principles definition.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.**
The first-principles definition (also called the **Newton quotient** or **limit definition of the derivative**) is:

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x + h) - f(x)}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f(x) = x^2$.**
We need $f(x+h)$, which means we replace every occurrence of $x$ in $f(x)$ with $(x+h)$:

$$
f(x+h) = (x+h)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Expand $(x+h)^2$ using the binomial identity.**
We apply the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$
f(x+h) = x^2 + 2xh + h^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the difference quotient.**
Now we form the numerator $f(x+h) - f(x)$:

$$
f(x+h) - f(x) = \left(x^2 + 2xh + h^2\right) - x^2
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator.**
The $x^2$ terms cancel:

$$
f(x+h) - f(x) = 2xh + h^2
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by $h$.**
The denominator in the difference quotient is $h$, so:

$$
\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} = \frac{2xh + h^2}{h}
$$

**Step 7 — Factor $h$ from the numerator and cancel.**
We factor out $h$ from the right-hand side:

$$
\frac{2xh + h^2}{h} = \frac{h(2x + h)}{h} = 2x + h
$$

**Step 8 — Apply the limit $h \to 0$.**
With the $h$ simplified away, taking the limit is now trivial:

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} (2x + h) = 2x + 0 = 2x
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}(x^2) = 2x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Interpretation:** If $f(t) = t^2$ represents the area (in cm²) of a tissue as a function of time $t$ (in days), then $f'(t) = 2t$ tells us the tissue is **expanding at a rate of $2t$ cm²/day** — accelerating growth, typical of early tumour expansion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Derivative of $f(x) = e^{kx}$ (Exponential Life-Science Model)

**Problem:** Find $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{kx})$ for a constant $k$.

**Step 1 — Use the first-principles definition.**

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{k(x+h)} - e^{kx}}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the exponential law $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$.**

$$
e^{k(x+h)} = e^{kx + kh} = e^{kx} \cdot e^{kh}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $e^{kx}$ from the numerator.**

$$
\frac{e^{kx} \cdot e^{kh} - e^{kx}}{h} = e^{kx} \cdot \frac{e^{kh} - 1}{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the standard limit $\lim\limits_{h \to 0} \dfrac{e^{kh} - 1}{h} = k$.**
This is a well-known result from the series expansion $e^{kh} = 1 + kh + \dfrac{(kh)^2}{2!} + \cdots$.

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$
f'(x) = e^{kx} \cdot k = k \cdot e^{kx}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}(e^{kx}) = k \cdot e^{kx}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Life-Science Application:** The bacterial growth law $N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}$ has derivative $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = k \cdot N_0 e^{kt} = k \cdot N(t)$. The growth rate is **proportional to the current population** — the more bacteria there are, the faster they reproduce. This is the mathematical statement of Malthus's Law.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Derivative of $f(x) = \ln x$ (Logistic Decay Context)

**Problem:** Find $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x)$ for $x > 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply the first-principles definition.**

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\ln(x+h) - \ln x}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the logarithm subtraction rule $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$.**

$$
\frac{\ln(x+h) - \ln x}{h} = \frac{1}{h} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{x+h}{x}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the argument of the logarithm.**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{x+h}{x}\right) = \ln\left(1 + \frac{h}{x}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the limit.**

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{h}{x}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard limit $\lim\limits_{u \to 0} \dfrac{\ln(1+u)}{u} = 1$ with $u = h/x$.**

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{h}{x}\right) = \frac{1}{x} \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\ln(1 + h/x)}{h/x} = \frac{1}{x} \cdot 1
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \frac{1}{x}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Use:** Models of drug clearance in plasma often follow $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$. Taking the natural log, $\ln C(t) = \ln C_0 - kt$, gives a **linear** function whose slope $-k$ is the **rate constant of elimination** — central to pharmacokinetic dosing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Connecting Derivative to Life-Science Quantity

| Life-Science Quantity | Functional Form | Derivative | Physical / Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population size | $N(t)$ | $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | Net growth rate (births − deaths) |
| Drug concentration | $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC_0 e^{-kt}$ | Clearance rate |
| Enzyme velocity | $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | $\dfrac{dv}{d[S]}$ | Sensitivity to substrate |
| Tissue area | $A(t) = \pi r^2(t)$ | $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = 2\pi r \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}$ | Expansion rate |
| Neural signal | $V(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = V_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$ | Voltage change rate |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Static Function to Rate of Change

The following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the logical pipeline for solving any "rate of change" problem in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It traces how a static biological quantity is mathematically converted into a dynamic rate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Life Science Quantity Q(t)"] --> B["Identify the Function f(t)"]
    B --> C{"Is the function discrete or continuous?"}
    C -->|Discrete| D["Use Average Rate: f(b)-f(a) over b-a"]
    C -->|Continuous| E["Use Instantaneous Rate: f'(t)"]
    D --> F["Compute Numerical Slope"]
    E --> G["Choose Differentiation Method"]
    G --> G1["First Principles (Limit Definition)"]
    G --> G2["Standard Rules (Power, Product, Chain)"]
    G1 --> H["Evaluate f'(t) at required point"]
    G2 --> H
    F --> I["Interpret Sign of Result"]
    H --> I
    I --> J{"Sign of f'(t)?"}
    J -->|Positive| K["Q is INCREASING - e.g., growth"]
    J -->|Negative| L["Q is DECREASING - e.g., decay"]
    J -->|Zero| M["Q is at EQUILIBRIUM - e.g., steady state"]
    K --> N["END: Biological Interpretation"]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every KTU "rate of change" problem funnels through this pipeline. The most common error is jumping from $f(t)$ directly to the answer — skipping the **sign interpretation** step. Always close with a biological statement.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Modular Components of the Derivative Engine

The following diagram treats the derivative as a **modular engine** with input, processing blocks, and output. This is the most KTU-board-friendly way to organize the concept in your answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["INPUT BLOCK: f(t), t, Point of Interest"] --> DIFFSEL{"DIFFERENTIATION SELECTOR"}
    DIFFSEL -->|Power Law| PR["Power Rule: n*x^(n-1)"]
    DIFFSEL -->|Exponential| EX["Exponential Rule: k*e^(kt)"]
    DIFFSEL -->|Logarithmic| LG["Log Rule: 1/t"]
    DIFFSEL -->|Composite| CH["Chain Rule: f'(g) * g'"]
    DIFFSEL -->|Quotient| QU["Quotient Rule: (u'v-uv')/v^2"]
    PR --> EVAL["EVALUATION BLOCK: Substitute t = t0"]
    EX --> EVAL
    LG --> EVAL
    CH --> EVAL
    QU --> EVAL
    EVAL --> OUT["OUTPUT BLOCK: f'(t0) with Units"]
    OUT --> BIO["BIOLOGICAL MEANING BLOCK: Sign + Magnitude + Context"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** In KTU ESE answers, mirror this 4-block structure in your written solutions: (1) Identify inputs, (2) Choose rule, (3) Compute, (4) Interpret biologically. Examiners reward this clarity with full marks.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Diagram: Average vs. Instantaneous Rate

The figure below contrasts the two fundamental notions of rate that students frequently confuse in exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AVG["AVERAGE RATE OF CHANGE"] --> AVG1["Defined over an INTERVAL [a, b]"]
    AVG1 --> AVG2["Geometric meaning: SLOPE OF SECANT LINE"]
    AVG2 --> AVG3["Formula: f(b) - f(a) over b - a"]
    AVG3 --> AVG4["Use case: Mean growth over years"]

    INST["INSTANTANEOUS RATE OF CHANGE"] --> INST1["Defined at a SINGLE POINT x = a"]
    INST1 --> INST2["Geometric meaning: SLOPE OF TANGENT LINE"]
    INST2 --> INST3["Formula: limit as h to 0 of f(a+h)-f(a) over h"]
    INST3 --> INST4["Use case: Growth at exact moment t = t0"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** The **secant** connects two points; the **tangent** touches one. The derivative is the limit of secants becoming a tangent. Drawing both lines on a rough graph during the exam wins easy method marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Define the rate of change of a function. Distinguish between average and instantaneous rates of change with a biological example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **rate of change of a function** $f(t)$ measures how fast the dependent variable changes with respect to the independent variable.

- **Average Rate of Change** over $[a, b]$ is given by:

$$
\frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}
$$

- **Instantaneous Rate of Change** at $t = a$ is the limit:

$$
f'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}
$$

**Biological Example:** If $N(t)$ denotes the number of bacteria in a culture at time $t$, then $\frac{N(b) - N(a)}{b - a}$ gives the **mean growth rate** of the colony between days $a$ and $b$, whereas $N'(a)$ gives the **growth rate at the precise moment** $t = a$ — useful for monitoring exponential expansion.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for biological example.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**State the first-principles definition of the derivative. Hence compute $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^3)$ using the same.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}
$$

**Computation (2 Marks):**
Let $f(x) = x^3$. Then $f(x+h) = (x+h)^3 = x^3 + 3x^2 h + 3x h^2 + h^3$.

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{x^3 + 3x^2 h + 3x h^2 + h^3 - x^3}{h}
$$

$$
= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{3x^2 h + 3x h^2 + h^3}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} (3x^2 + 3x h + h^2) = 3x^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In Module 2 questions on Rates of Change, students typically lose marks in three places: (1) **forgetting the biological interpretation** of the derivative's sign — examiners demand a contextual comment, not just a number; (2) **sign errors** when applying the quotient rule in enzyme kinetics problems; (3) **missing units** in the final answer (e.g., *cells/day*, *mg/hour*). Always close with a units-aware biological sentence.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** The population of a species in a controlled habitat follows the model $P(t) = 500 + 200t - 5t^2$, where $P$ is the population size and $t$ is the time in years. **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Find the rate of change of population with respect to time.
   (ii) At what time is the population growing the fastest?
   (iii) Interpret the result biologically.

**(b)** A drug is administered intravenously. Its concentration in the bloodstream (in mg/L) at time $t$ (in hours) is given by $C(t) = 80 e^{-0.4 t}$. **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Find the rate at which the drug concentration is changing at any time $t$.
   (ii) Determine the rate of change at $t = 2$ hours.
   (iii) Interpret the sign and magnitude of the rate biologically.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — (7 Marks)**

**(i) Rate of change (3 Marks):**

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(500 + 200t - 5t^2) = 0 + 200 - 10t = 200 - 10t
$$

**(ii) Time of fastest growth (2 Marks):**
The population grows fastest when $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is **maximum**. Since $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = 200 - 10t$ is a linearly decreasing function of $t$, its maximum is at the **earliest time**, i.e., $t = 0$ years, where the rate is $200$ individuals/year.

Alternatively, setting the second derivative to zero:
$\dfrac{d^2 P}{dt^2} = -10 < 0$, confirming that the rate is **monotonically decreasing** — there is no interior maximum, only the boundary maximum at $t = 0$.

**(iii) Biological interpretation (2 Marks):**
At $t = 0$ (start of observation), the population is growing at its peak rate of **200 individuals per year**. As time progresses, the growth rate **slows down** due to resource limitations (captured by the $-5t^2$ term in the model). The population will eventually decline once $t > 20$ years, when $\dfrac{dP}{dt} < 0$.

---

**Part (b) — (7 Marks)**

**(i) Rate of change at time $t$ (3 Marks):**

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(80 e^{-0.4t}\right) = 80 \cdot (-0.4) \cdot e^{-0.4t} = -32 e^{-0.4t}
$$

**(ii) Rate of change at $t = 2$ hours (2 Marks):**

$$
\frac{dC}{dt}\bigg|_{t=2} = -32 e^{-0.4 \cdot 2} = -32 e^{-0.8}
$$

Since $e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493$:

$$
\frac{dC}{dt}\bigg|_{t=2} \approx -32 \cdot 0.4493 \approx -14.38 \text{ mg/L per hour}
$$

**(iii) Biological interpretation (2 Marks):**
The **negative sign** indicates that the drug concentration is **decreasing** — the body is metabolizing and clearing the drug. The **magnitude** ($14.38$ mg/L/hr) tells us the drug is being eliminated rapidly at the 2-hour mark. In clinical practice, this rate of clearance is critical for determining **dosage intervals** to maintain therapeutic concentration.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** A bacterial culture grows according to $N(t) = 1000 \cdot e^{0.3 t}$, where $t$ is in hours. **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Find $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ at $t = 0$ and at $t = 5$.
   (ii) Compare the two rates and explain the biological significance.

**(b)** For the function $f(x) = \dfrac{3x + 2}{x - 1}$, $x \neq 1$: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Find $f'(x)$ using the quotient rule.
   (ii) Find the rate of change at $x = 4$.
   (iii) State the domain of $f$ and comment on the behaviour of the function near the excluded point $x = 1$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — (7 Marks)**

**(i) Derivative calculation (4 Marks):**

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(1000 \cdot e^{0.3t}\right) = 1000 \cdot 0.3 \cdot e^{0.3t} = 300 \cdot e^{0.3t}
$$

At $t = 0$:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} = 300 \cdot e^{0} = 300 \cdot 1 = 300 \text{ bacteria/hour}
$$

At $t = 5$:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t=5} = 300 \cdot e^{1.5} \approx 300 \cdot 4.4817 \approx 1344.5 \text{ bacteria/hour}
$$

**(ii) Comparison and biological interpretation (3 Marks):**
The growth rate at $t = 5$ hours is approximately **4.48 times** the initial rate ($300$ vs. $1344.5$ bacteria/hour). This is the defining feature of **exponential (Malthusian) growth**: the rate itself grows exponentially. The larger the existing population, the more cells are dividing, and so the rate of increase compounds. Biologically, this represents unchecked proliferation — a hallmark of **microbial contamination in food**, **tumour growth in early-stage cancer**, or **invasive species colonization** — all of which require early intervention before the rate becomes overwhelming.

---

**Part (b) — (7 Marks)**

**(i) Apply the quotient rule (4 Marks):**
With $u = 3x + 2$ and $v = x - 1$, we have $u' = 3$ and $v' = 1$:

$$
f'(x) = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2} = \frac{3(x - 1) - (3x + 2)(1)}{(x - 1)^2}
$$

**Numerator simplification:**

$$
3(x - 1) - (3x + 2) = 3x - 3 - 3x - 2 = -5
$$

Therefore:

$$
f'(x) = \frac{-5}{(x - 1)^2}
$$

**(ii) Rate of change at $x = 4$ (2 Marks):**

$$
f'(4) = \frac{-5}{(4 - 1)^2} = \frac{-5}{9} \approx -0.556
$$

**(iii) Domain and behaviour (1 Mark):**
The domain is $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{1\}$ (all real numbers except $1$). As $x \to 1$, the denominator $(x-1)^2 \to 0^+$, so $f'(x) \to -\infty$. The function has a **vertical asymptote** at $x = 1$, meaning the rate of change becomes unboundedly negative there — useful in life sciences for modelling **singular events** like sudden population crashes or threshold drug toxicities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Rates of Change**

- ✅ **Rate of change** = derivative $f'(t)$; measures how fast a quantity varies with respect to its independent variable.
- ✅ **Average rate** uses the secant slope formula $\dfrac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$.
- ✅ **Instantaneous rate** uses the limit definition $f'(x) = \lim\limits_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$.
- ✅ **First-principles derivation** of $x^2$, $x^3$, $e^{kx}$, and $\ln x$ is a KTU 2024 board favorite — practice all four.
- ✅ **Standard rules:** Power, Product, Quotient, Chain — all must be memorized.
- ✅ **Sign of derivative** dictates biological meaning: $+ve$ = growth, $-ve$ = decay, $0$ = equilibrium.
- ✅ **Exponential growth** $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = kN$ is the **Malthusian Law** — a core life-science model.
- ✅ **Drug decay** $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC$ is a **first-order elimination** model.
- ✅ **Always include units** in the final answer (e.g., cells/day, mg/L/hr).
- ✅ **Always close with a biological interpretation** — examiners award full method marks only when context is given.
- ✅ **Logistic model** $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{K}\right)$ is the next-step upgrade beyond exponential — recognize its structure even if it appears in Module 3.
- ✅ For the KTU ESE 14-mark questions, the **2-sub-part structure** (7 + 7 marks) is sacrosanct — never write a single 14-mark answer without sub-headings.
- ✅ When in doubt, **draw a graph** of the function and indicate the tangent line — KTU examiners award method marks liberally for visuals.

> **Final Note:** The topic of Rates of Change is the **analytical engine** that powers all subsequent modules in GDMAT101. Master it now, and differential equations, optimization, and population modeling in later modules will feel like natural extensions.
